collection: Indian History — Historic Figures & True History
purpose: Record-based, child-safe illustrated retellings of India's history (ancient to freedom struggle): kings, empires, freedom fighters, and unsung heroes. Every episode carries real primary/scholarly citations per the sourcing standard in ROADMAP.md.
sourcing_standard: Primary records first (epigraphy, chronicles, archives, court statements, speeches); Wikipedia only as an index. Legend labelled as tradition in each episode's 'notes'.

episodes.title: Rani Lakshmibai's Stand at Kotah-ki-Serai
episodes.logline: When Jhansi fell and Gwalior became the next turning point, Lakshmibai rode with her companions to keep hope alive. Her final stand is remembered as a moment when courage outlasted fear.
episodes.moral: Bravery means protecting your people and your duty even when the road is hardest.

sources.citation: G. W. Forrest, A History of the Indian Mutiny; Reviewed and Illustrated from Original Documents, vol. 3, W. Blackwood, 1904, chapters on the Central India campaign
sources.note: Supports the Central India campaign, Jhansi, Gwalior, and British accounts of Lakshmibai's final resistance.

sources.citation: D. V. Tahmankar, The Ranee of Jhansi, Thacker & Co., 1958
sources.note: Synthesizes Indian and British accounts of Lakshmibai's leadership and last days.

episodes.notes: Her death in the Gwalior/Kotah-ki-Serai fighting is documented; the popular image of her riding with her child tied to her back is a later tradition and is not used as a key claim.

episodes.title: Mangal Pandey at Barrackpore
episodes.logline: At Barrackpore in March 1857, sepoy Mangal Pandey stepped into a tense parade ground and refused to be silent about the fears spreading through the army. His action became one of the sparks that people later connected with the great uprising.
episodes.moral: A single act of conscience can awaken many others, but history must remember it truthfully.

sources.citation: Proceedings of the General Court Martial of Sepoy Mangal Pandey, Barrackpore, 6 April 1857, Military Department records, India Office Records/British Library
sources.note: Primary proceedings for the Barrackpore incident and the formal charge against Pandey.

sources.citation: Rudrangshu Mukherjee, Mangal Pandey: Brave Martyr or Accidental Hero?, Penguin/Viking, 2005
sources.note: Examines the evidence, memory, and later nationalist interpretation of Pandey's role.

sources.citation: S. N. Sen, Eighteen Fifty-Seven, Publications Division, Government of India, 1957
sources.note: Places Barrackpore within the wider chronology of the 1857 uprising.

episodes.notes: The Barrackpore event and court martial are documented; later titles such as 'first martyr' are commemorative interpretations, so the episode avoids overclaiming direct causation.

episodes.title: Bahadur Shah Zafar and the Red Fort Call
episodes.logline: In 1857, the elderly poet-emperor Bahadur Shah Zafar became a symbol around whom many rebel soldiers and citizens gathered. In the Red Fort, his name carried a fragile hope of unity across a divided city.
episodes.moral: Leadership can be symbolic as well as military when people need a shared banner.

sources.citation: Trial by European Military Commission of Emperor Bahadur Shah, reproduced in Two Historic Trials in Red Fort, Publications Division/Government of India edition
sources.note: Documents the charges, proceedings, and the Red Fort trial after the fall of Delhi.

sources.citation: K. C. Yadav, ed., Delhi in 1857, vol. 1, National Archives-related document collection
sources.note: Collects contemporary records and rebel/official material on Delhi during the uprising.

sources.citation: William Dalrymple, The Last Mughal: The Fall of a Dynasty, Delhi 1857, Bloomsbury, 2006
sources.note: Scholarly narrative using Persian, Urdu, and British archival sources for Zafar's symbolic role.

episodes.notes: Zafar's practical authority over all rebel forces was limited and contested; the episode treats him as a unifying symbol rather than a commander of every action.

episodes.title: Kunwar Singh Returns to Jagdispur
episodes.logline: In his eighties, Kunwar Singh led a daring return toward Jagdispur after months of fighting across Bihar and nearby regions. His comeback showed that age could not dim resolve when homeland and honour called.
episodes.moral: Courage is not measured by youth, but by steadfastness and strategy.

sources.citation: William Tayler, The Defence of the Arrah House, 1857, W. Thacker & Co., 1858
sources.note: Contemporary colonial account of the Shahabad/Arrah conflict involving Kunwar Singh's forces.

sources.citation: G. W. Forrest, A History of the Indian Mutiny; Reviewed and Illustrated from Original Documents, vol. 3, W. Blackwood, 1904, sections on mutiny in Western Behar and Jagdispur
sources.note: Supports Kunwar Singh's campaign movements and the recovery of Jagdispur.

sources.citation: S. N. Sen, Eighteen Fifty-Seven, Publications Division, Government of India, 1957
sources.note: Provides a national synthesis of Kunwar Singh's role in the revolt.

episodes.notes: The return to Jagdispur is documented. The dramatic story that he sacrificed an injured hand to the Ganga is widely repeated as tradition and is not treated as verified fact.

episodes.title: Tatya Tope's Long March
episodes.region: Central India, Rajasthan, and the Deccan routes
episodes.logline: After major rebel centres fell, Tatya Tope kept moving with small forces, choosing surprise, speed, and endurance over surrender. His long campaign turned retreat into a lesson in resilience.
episodes.moral: Ingenuity can keep hope alive when strength must be used carefully.

sources.citation: G. W. Forrest, A History of the Indian Mutiny; Reviewed and Illustrated from Original Documents, vol. 3, W. Blackwood, 1904, 'Pursuit of Tantia Topee'
sources.note: Documents the long pursuit and movements attributed to Tatya Tope.

sources.citation: Proceedings of the trial of Tantia Topee/Tatya Tope, Shivpuri, April 1859, Military Department records, Government of India selections
sources.note: Primary judicial record for the end of his campaign and the charges against him.

sources.citation: S. N. Sen, Eighteen Fifty-Seven, Publications Division, Government of India, 1957
sources.note: Synthesizes Tatya Tope's role after the fall of Gwalior and during the mobile campaign.

episodes.notes: His mobile campaign is well documented in British pursuit records; exact motives and some betrayal details depend heavily on colonial accounts and should be framed cautiously.

episodes.title: Tilak Speaks in Court
episodes.logline: In a packed Bombay courtroom in 1908, Bal Gangadhar Tilak answered a sedition case with calm words about duty, conscience, and public life. Even a sentence against him could not quiet the idea of swaraj.
episodes.moral: Truthful speech and courage before authority can strengthen a whole movement.

sources.citation: Full & Authentic Report of the Tilak Trial, 1908, The Mahratta/Bombay High Court proceedings, 1908
sources.note: Verbatim court record of Tilak's 1908 sedition trial and statement.

sources.citation: Bal Gangadhar Tilak, Bal Gangadhar Tilak: His Writings and Speeches, with appreciation by Aurobindo Ghose, Ganesh & Co., 1918
sources.note: Collected speeches and writings supporting Tilak's public ideas on swaraj and political education.

episodes.notes: The famous line 'Swaraj is my birthright...' is associated with Tilak's Home Rule-era speeches, but variants exist; this episode centers on the documented 1908 trial rather than a single slogan.

episodes.title: Lala Lajpat Rai Faces the Simon Commission
episodes.logline: When the Simon Commission came to Lahore without Indian members, Lala Lajpat Rai helped lead a disciplined protest. His courage turned a painful day into a call for dignity and self-respect.
episodes.moral: Peaceful protest can show great strength when it is disciplined and principled.

sources.citation: The Tribune (Lahore), reports on the Lahore Simon Commission protest, 31 October 1928, and Lajpat Rai's death, 18 November 1928
sources.note: Contemporary newspaper coverage of the protest and its aftermath.

sources.citation: Jawaharlal Nehru, An Autobiography, John Lane/The Bodley Head, 1936, chapters on the Simon Commission period
sources.note: Contemporary nationalist recollection of the anti-Simon protests and Lajpat Rai's place in them.

sources.citation: B. R. Nanda, Lajpat Rai: The Man and His Ideas, Oxford University Press, 1965
sources.note: Scholarly biography discussing the Lahore protest and its political impact.

episodes.notes: The protest and subsequent death are documented. The exact wording of the often-quoted 'every blow...' line is treated cautiously because it appears most clearly in later nationalist retellings.

episodes.title: Bagha Jatin by the Buribalam
episodes.era: Revolutionary nationalism during World War I, 1915
episodes.logline: Near the Buribalam river, Bagha Jatin and a small band chose to protect one another while a larger force closed in. Their stand became a quiet emblem of loyalty, planning, and sacrifice.
episodes.moral: True bravery includes loyalty to companions and responsibility for a larger cause.

sources.citation: Report of the Sedition Committee, 1918 (Rowlatt Report), Government of India, Calcutta, 1918, sections on Bengal revolutionary organisations and the German-Indian conspiracy
sources.note: Official contemporary account of revolutionary networks connected with Jatindranath Mukherjee.

sources.citation: Peter Heehs, The Bomb in Bengal: The Rise of Revolutionary Terrorism in India, 1900-1910, Oxford University Press, 1993
sources.note: Background on Bengal revolutionary organisations that shaped Bagha Jatin's milieu.

sources.citation: Sudhir Kumar Mitra, Bagha Jatin, biographical study, 20th-century Bengali nationalist history
sources.note: Supports the Balasore/Buribalam episode and later remembrance.

episodes.notes: The Balasore encounter is documented in official and biographical sources; speeches or last words attributed to Jatin are often commemorative and should be treated as tradition unless independently sourced.

episodes.title: Khudiram Bose's Young Resolve
episodes.logline: As a teenager in a high-stakes revolutionary case, Khudiram Bose faced the court with striking composure. His youth made many Indians ask what kind of courage a country could inspire in its children and students.
episodes.moral: Courage must be joined with wisdom, because young hearts can carry very heavy dreams.

sources.citation: Amiya K. Samanta, ed., Alipore Bomb Trial, 1908-1910, Government of West Bengal/State Archives document collection
sources.note: Publishes trial documents and context for the Bengal revolutionary cases connected with Muzaffarpur.

sources.citation: Peter Heehs, The Bomb in Bengal: The Rise of Revolutionary Terrorism in India, 1900-1910, Oxford University Press, 1993
sources.note: Scholarly account of the Muzaffarpur case and Khudiram's role.

sources.citation: The Bengalee and Amrita Bazar Patrika, Calcutta, May-August 1908 coverage of the Muzaffarpur case and trial
sources.note: Contemporary press context for public reaction to the case.

episodes.notes: The Muzaffarpur case and trial are documented; stories of Khudiram smiling in his final moments are common in memory but should be presented gently and marked as tradition if used.

episodes.title: The Kakori Promise
episodes.figure: Ashfaqulla Khan and Ram Prasad Bismil
episodes.logline: After the Kakori train action, Ashfaqulla Khan and Ram Prasad Bismil faced trial with steadfast friendship and a shared dream of freedom. Their letters and poems turned a courtroom story into a lesson in unity.
episodes.moral: Friendship across backgrounds can become a powerful force for a just cause.

sources.citation: Kakori Conspiracy Case judgment, Chief Court of Oudh/Lucknow, 1927
sources.note: Primary judicial record for the Kakori case, accused persons, and sentences.

sources.citation: Ram Prasad Bismil, Atmakatha/Autobiography, written in prison and published posthumously in Hindi editions
sources.note: First-person account of Bismil's ideals, comradeship, and reflections.

sources.citation: Jail Notebook and letters of Ashfaqulla Khan, preserved in published family/nationalist collections
sources.note: Supports Ashfaqulla Khan's reflections and dignified tone in captivity.

episodes.notes: The Kakori case is documented by court records. The episode avoids operational detail and focuses on documented comradeship, letters, and ideals; some popular dialogues are later tradition.

episodes.title: Bhagat Singh Makes the Deaf Hear
episodes.logline: In the Central Legislative Assembly, Bhagat Singh and B. K. Dutt chose a dramatic protest meant to make a message heard rather than to harm bystanders. In court, Singh used clear words to explain why ideas cannot be locked away.
episodes.moral: A brave message is strongest when it is guided by purpose and responsibility.

sources.citation: Statement of Bhagat Singh and Batukeshwar Dutt in the Assembly Bomb Case, Sessions Court, Delhi, 6 June 1929
sources.note: Primary explanation of the Assembly action and the phrase 'to make the deaf hear.'

sources.citation: Malwinder Jit Singh Waraich, ed., Complete Tribunal Proceedings: Lahore Conspiracy Case, document collection
sources.note: Publishes tribunal proceedings and related documents for Bhagat Singh and his comrades.

sources.citation: D. N. Gupta, ed., Bhagat Singh: Select Speeches and Writings, National Book Trust/People's Publishing editions
sources.note: Collects Singh's statements, letters, and essays for his political ideas.

episodes.notes: The Assembly incident and court statement are documented; the child-safe version stresses intent, responsibility, and ideas rather than explosive detail.

episodes.title: Rajguru and Sukhdev Stand Together
episodes.logline: Rajguru's daring and Sukhdev's organizing strength met in the same difficult trial as Bhagat Singh. Their shared resolve reminds us that history is made by teams, not only by the most famous name.
episodes.moral: A movement depends on many kinds of courage: action, planning, loyalty, and calm endurance.

sources.citation: Lahore Conspiracy Case Tribunal judgment, 7 October 1930, reproduced in Gurdev Singh Sidhu, The Hanging of Bhagat Singh: Complete Judgment and Other Documents
sources.note: Primary legal record naming Rajguru and Sukhdev and summarizing the prosecution case.

sources.citation: Malwinder Jit Singh Waraich, ed., Complete Tribunal Proceedings: Lahore Conspiracy Case, 2017 document collection
sources.note: Provides proceedings and documents for the tribunal and the accused revolutionaries.

sources.citation: Kama Maclean, A Revolutionary History of Interwar India: Violence, Image, Voice and Text, Oxford University Press, 2015
sources.note: Scholarly analysis of HSRA networks, memory, and public image.

episodes.notes: The trial and association with Bhagat Singh are documented. Individual private conversations among the three are often reconstructed later and should not be presented as verbatim fact.

episodes.title: Chandrashekhar Azad at Alfred Park
episodes.logline: At Alfred Park, Chandrashekhar Azad faced encirclement while trying to keep a comrade safe. The story endures because he had built a life around one fierce promise: to remain Azad, free in spirit.
episodes.moral: Freedom begins with inner resolve, but true courage also protects others.

sources.citation: United Provinces Police reports on the Alfred Park encounter, Allahabad, 27 February 1931, Home/Police Department records
sources.note: Primary official record for the encounter and location.

sources.citation: Kama Maclean, A Revolutionary History of Interwar India: Violence, Image, Voice and Text, Oxford University Press, 2015
sources.note: Scholarly treatment of Azad, HSRA networks, and revolutionary memory.

sources.citation: Shiv Verma, Sansmritiyan/Memoirs of a Revolutionary, Hindi editions, recollections of HSRA colleagues
sources.note: Participant recollections about Azad's personality and role in the movement.

episodes.notes: The Alfred Park encounter is documented, but details of Azad's final words and exact last choices vary across police reports and nationalist memories; present the core facts without graphic depiction.

episodes.title: Subhas Bose Calls 'Chalo Delhi'
episodes.figure: Subhas Chandra Bose and the Indian National Army
episodes.region: Singapore, Rangoon, and the India-Burma front
episodes.logline: Subhas Chandra Bose stood before the Indian National Army and gave a ringing call toward Delhi. For soldiers far from home, his words turned longing into discipline, service, and sacrifice.
episodes.moral: A leader's words can organize scattered hopes into purposeful action.

sources.citation: S. A. Ayer, ed., Selected Speeches of Subhas Chandra Bose, Publications Division, Government of India, 1962
sources.note: Contains Bose's INA speeches, including calls such as 'Chalo Delhi' and appeals for sacrifice.

sources.citation: INA papers and Azad Hind Government records, National Archives of India, files on the Provisional Government of Azad Hind and INA broadcasts
sources.note: Archival basis for INA organization, proclamations, and wartime messaging.

sources.citation: Two Historic Trials in Red Fort, account of the INA trial of Shah Nawaz Khan, P. K. Sahgal, and G. S. Dhillon, Government of India/Red Fort trial documents
sources.note: Shows how INA service was later examined in court and publicly remembered.

episodes.notes: Bose's speeches and INA records are documented. The episode avoids battlefield violence and focuses on mobilizing words, discipline, and the symbolic march toward Delhi.

episodes.title: Sardar Patel and the Bardoli Test
episodes.logline: When Bardoli's farmers faced a heavy revenue demand, Vallabhbhai Patel helped them organize patiently, village by village. Their disciplined unity won respect and gave Patel the name 'Sardar.'
episodes.moral: Firm organization and non-violent discipline can turn ordinary people into a powerful community.

sources.citation: Mahadev Desai, The Story of Bardoli: Being a History of the Bardoli Satyagraha of 1928 and Its Sequel, Navajivan Publishing House, 1929
sources.note: Near-contemporary account by Gandhi's secretary documenting Patel's role and the farmers' discipline.

sources.citation: The Collected Works of Mahatma Gandhi, vol. 42, letters and articles on Bardoli, 1928
sources.note: Primary correspondence and commentary on the Bardoli campaign and Patel's leadership.

sources.citation: Rajmohan Gandhi, Patel: A Life, Navajivan/Oxford editions, 1990
sources.note: Scholarly biography placing Bardoli in Patel's wider public life.

episodes.notes: Bardoli's campaign and settlement are well documented; the episode highlights verified non-violent organization rather than turning Patel into a lone saviour.

episodes.title: Begum Hazrat Mahal's Proclamation
episodes.logline: As regent in Awadh, Begum Hazrat Mahal spoke for her young son and for people anxious about their homes, rights, and future. Her proclamation asked listeners to judge promises carefully and stand with dignity.
episodes.moral: Wise courage includes speaking up for justice with dignity and care for all people.

sources.citation: Proclamation of Begum Hazrat Mahal, Lucknow, 31 December 1858, printed in British Parliamentary Papers/East India proclamations collections
sources.note: Primary proclamation responding to Queen Victoria's 1858 proclamation and calling Awadh to vigilance.

sources.citation: Rudrangshu Mukherjee, Awadh in Revolt, 1857-1858: A Study of Popular Resistance, Oxford University Press, 1984
sources.note: Scholarly analysis of Awadh resistance and the Begum's political role.

sources.citation: S. N. Sen, Eighteen Fifty-Seven, Publications Division, Government of India, 1957, chapters on Awadh
sources.note: Government-published historical synthesis based on official and Indian sources.

episodes.notes: The proclamation is documented; avoid communal framing by presenting her concerns as political, civic, and protective of Awadh's people rather than as hostility to any community.

episodes.title: Alluri Sitarama Raju's Hill Country Strategy
episodes.region: Godavari Agency and Eastern Ghats, Andhra region
episodes.logline: In the forested hills of the Godavari Agency, Alluri Sitarama Raju moved swiftly with tribal supporters who opposed harsh forest rules and local oppression. His strategy showed how knowledge of land and people could challenge a much larger power.
episodes.moral: Respect for local communities and the land can become a source of courage and strategy.

sources.citation: Government of Madras, Public/Confidential Department proceedings on the Rampa Rebellion, 1922-1924, Tamil Nadu Archives/Madras Presidency records
sources.note: Primary administrative records for the rebellion, raids, pursuit, and suppression.

sources.citation: David Arnold, 'Rebellious Hillmen: The Gudem-Rampa Risings, 1839-1924,' in Ranajit Guha, ed., Subaltern Studies I, Oxford University Press, 1982
sources.note: Scholarly study of the Gudem-Rampa risings and Alluri's role among hill communities.

sources.citation: K. S. Singh, Tribal Movements in India, vol. 1, Manohar, 1982, sections on the Rampa rebellion
sources.note: Places Alluri's movement within broader tribal resistance history.

episodes.notes: The rebellion and Alluri's leadership are documented, but miraculous powers and prophetic details in folklore are tradition and should be labelled as such if ever used.

episodes.title: Surya Sen Raises the Chittagong Flag
episodes.logline: Teacher Surya Sen and his young comrades carried out a daring Chittagong plan and briefly raised the national flag as a promise of freedom. The episode follows the courage, discipline, and responsibility behind that risky midnight mission.
episodes.moral: Courage needs planning, teamwork, and care for a larger cause.

sources.citation: Kalpana Dutt, Chittagong Armoury Raiders: Reminiscences, People's Publishing House, 1945/1979
sources.note: Participant memoir for Surya Sen's leadership, the 18 April 1930 Chittagong action, and the later underground phase.

sources.citation: Manini Chatterjee, Do and Die: The Chittagong Uprising 1930-34, Penguin, 1999
sources.note: Modern scholarly reconstruction of the Chittagong uprising and Surya Sen's role.

episodes.notes: The Chittagong raid and flag-raising are documented; heroic dialogue and exact scene details often come from memoir and later retellings, so dramatization should stay modest.

episodes.title: Aruna Asaf Ali at Gowalia Tank
episodes.logline: When senior leaders were arrested during Quit India, Aruna Asaf Ali stepped forward at Gowalia Tank Maidan and helped keep the public spirit alive. Her raised flag became a sign that courage could continue even when leaders were taken away.
episodes.moral: When a moment asks for responsibility, ordinary citizens can become leaders.

sources.citation: Aruna Asaf Ali, The Resurgence of Indian Women, Radiant Publishers, 1991
sources.note: Aruna Asaf Ali's reflections on women's public role and the nationalist movement.

sources.citation: Bipan Chandra, Mridula Mukherjee, Aditya Mukherjee, K. N. Panikkar and Sucheta Mahajan, India's Struggle for Independence, Penguin, 1989, chapter on Quit India
sources.note: Synthesizes the Quit India arrests, underground activity, and Aruna Asaf Ali's symbolic role.

episodes.notes: The 9 August 1942 Gowalia Tank flag incident is documented; later descriptions sometimes heighten the drama, so visual treatment should emphasize resolve, not confrontation.

episodes.title: Kamaladevi Sells Salt for Freedom
episodes.logline: During the Salt Satyagraha, Kamaladevi Chattopadhyay turned a simple pinch of salt into a bold public lesson about unfair laws. Her action reminds children that small things can carry big meaning when done with courage.
episodes.moral: Even a small symbol can become powerful when it stands for justice.

sources.citation: Kamaladevi Chattopadhyay, Inner Recesses Outer Spaces: Memoirs, Navrang, 1986
sources.note: Memoir source for her civil-disobedience participation and public activism.

sources.citation: Nico Slate, Art of Freedom: Kamaladevi Chattopadhyay and the Making of Modern India, University of Pittsburgh Press, 2024
sources.note: Recent scholarly biography covering her Salt Satyagraha work and later nation-building.

episodes.notes: The salt-sale story is part of documented civil-disobedience memory; exact market-scene dialogue should be treated as reconstructed rather than quoted.

episodes.title: Sarojini Naidu's Dharasana Promise
episodes.logline: After Gandhi was arrested, Sarojini Naidu helped guide volunteers at Dharasana with discipline and kindness. The episode shows a poet-leader reminding people that courage also means self-control.
episodes.moral: True strength can be gentle, disciplined, and brave at the same time.

sources.citation: Webb Miller, United Press dispatches from Dharasana, May 1930, reprinted in contemporary international newspapers including The Manchester Guardian and U.S. press
sources.note: Contemporary reporting on Dharasana and Naidu's leadership among the volunteers.

sources.citation: Sarojini Naidu, Speeches and Writings of Sarojini Naidu, G. A. Natesan & Co., Madras, 1918
sources.note: Primary speeches showing her public language of unity, courage, and civic duty.

episodes.notes: Dharasana involved harsh repression, but the child-safe episode should show disciplined marching, waiting, and moral courage without depicting injuries.

episodes.title: Prakasam Faces the Simon Protest
episodes.logline: During the Simon Commission protests in Madras, Tanguturi Prakasam stood firm when crowds were ordered back. His calm defiance won him the name Andhra Kesari, the Lion of Andhra.
episodes.moral: Standing firm for a just cause is strongest when it stays calm and principled.

sources.citation: Tanguturi Prakasam, Naa Jeevita Yatra, autobiographical volumes in Telugu, Andhra publishing editions
sources.note: Prakasam's own account of his public life and freedom-struggle work.

sources.citation: B. Pattabhi Sitaramayya, The History of the Indian National Congress, 1885-1935, vol. 1, Working Committee-authorized edition, 1935
sources.note: Context for Congress protests against the Simon Commission.

episodes.notes: The Madras protest and Prakasam's Andhra Kesari epithet are well known; details of the exact confrontation should be handled without showing weapons or injury.

episodes.title: Bharati's Fearless Song
episodes.logline: In Pondicherry, Subramania Bharati used poems and journalism to imagine a free, equal India. His fearless songs helped ordinary readers feel that freedom belonged in their own voices.
episodes.moral: Words can awaken courage when they are truthful and generous.

sources.citation: Subramania Bharati, Poems of Subramania Bharati and Chosen Poems and Prose, English renderings of selected Tamil writings
sources.note: Primary literary texts for Bharati's patriotic, social-reform, and equality themes.

sources.citation: Prema Nandakumar, Subramania Bharati, Sahitya Akademi/Indian Writers series editions
sources.note: Biographical and literary context for Bharati's Pondicherry years and nationalist writing.

episodes.notes: Bharati's poems are primary evidence for his ideals; specific claims about songs being sung at a particular protest need separate citation before use.

episodes.title: Deshbandhu's Courtroom Defence
episodes.logline: In the Alipore trial, lawyer Chittaranjan Das argued with patience and skill for Aurobindo Ghose's defence. The courtroom story shows that courage can also live in careful words and fairness before the law.
episodes.moral: Justice needs clear thinking, courage, and respect for truth.

sources.citation: Bejai Krishna Bose, The Alipore Bomb Case, contemporary/early account of the trial proceedings
sources.note: Trial-focused source for the case and Das's defence work.

sources.citation: Hemendranath Das Gupta, Deshbandhu Chittaranjan Das, Publications Division/biographical editions
sources.note: Biography covering Das's legal career, public leadership, and the Alipore defence.

episodes.notes: The Alipore trial and acquittal of Aurobindo are documented; the episode should avoid sensational details of the case and focus on advocacy and justice.

episodes.title: Bipin Chandra Pal Refuses the Witness Box
episodes.logline: When asked to give evidence in a sedition case connected with nationalist journalism, Bipin Chandra Pal refused to help a prosecution he believed was unjust and accepted imprisonment. His stand shows conscience choosing a hard path.
episodes.moral: Conscience means accepting consequences when you believe truth is at stake.

sources.citation: Bipin Chandra Pal, Memories of My Life and Times, Modern Book Agency/early editions
sources.note: Pal's own account of his political life and principles during the Swadeshi era.

sources.citation: Sedition Committee Report, 1918, Government of India, discussion of Bande Mataram-era prosecutions and revolutionary-nationalist politics
sources.note: Official context for sedition prosecutions and nationalist press activity in Bengal.

episodes.notes: Pal's refusal and imprisonment for contempt are documented; present it as a legal-moral stand, not as contempt for people in the courtroom.

episodes.title: Abbakka's Refusal at Ullal
episodes.era: Coastal resistance to Portuguese expansion, 16th century
episodes.logline: When a powerful fleet came to pressure Ullal, Queen Abbakka chose negotiation without surrendering her people's freedom. Her coastal stand shows a small port-kingdom defending its dignity on the Arabian Sea.
episodes.moral: Courage can be as firm in refusing unfair demands as it is in battle.

sources.citation: K. Abhishankar (ed.), Karnataka State Gazetteer: South Kanara District, Government of Karnataka, chapter II, section on Portuguese intrusions and the Ullala queen
sources.note: Identifies Abbakkadevi, the 'Queen of Ullala', as a persistent opponent of Portuguese expansion who stopped paying tribute and opposed them around Mangalore.

sources.citation: Frederick C. Danvers, The Portuguese in India, vol. 1, W. H. Allen, 1894, entry for 1555 under Dom Alvaro da Silveira and the Queen of Olala
sources.note: Portuguese-record-based narrative for the fleet of twenty-one sail sent against the queen after tribute was refused.

episodes.notes: Documented Portuguese sources use spellings such as Olala/Ullala and do not always give the personal name Abbakka; the identification with Abbakka Chowta comes from regional history and gazetteer tradition. The episode avoids later embellishments and focuses on the documented refusal of tribute and resistance to expansion.

episodes.title: Velu Nachiyar's Return to Sivaganga
episodes.logline: After years away from her throne, Velu Nachiyar gathered allies and returned to Sivaganga with careful planning. In popular Tamil memory, her commander Kuyili's sacrifice became the signal that courage and loyalty could open a path home.
episodes.moral: Preparation, friendship, and loyalty can restore hope after loss.

sources.citation: J. H. Nelson, The Madura Country: A Manual, compiled by order of the Madras Government, 1868, sections on Sivaganga/Ramnad politics
sources.note: Provides administrative-historical context for Sivaganga, local polities, and conflict in the region.

sources.citation: Lok Sabha Secretariat, Parliamentary record, 'Need to provide financial assistance ... Sivaganga Palace of Rani Velu Nachiar', 11 March 2002
sources.note: Shows official recognition of Rani Velu Nachiar and the Sivaganga palace as a heritage site.

episodes.notes: Velu Nachiyar's association with Sivaganga is historically grounded; the detailed Kuyili armoury-sacrifice episode is primarily preserved in later Tamil oral and commemorative tradition, so it is labelled as popular memory rather than archival certainty.

episodes.title: Kittur's Gate Holds Fast
episodes.logline: When Kittur's succession was challenged, Chennamma stood with her court and guards instead of quietly yielding the fort. The crisis at Kittur became an early regional stand against distant control.
episodes.moral: A leader protects her people's rights even when the opposing power seems much larger.

sources.citation: Gazetteer of the Bombay Presidency, vol. XXII: Dharwar, Government Central Press, 1884, historical section for 1824
sources.note: Records that Political Agent and Collector St John Thackeray was shot in a disturbance at Kittur after the chief died childless.

sources.citation: Sadashiv Vaidyar, Rani Chennamma of Kittur, National Book Trust, translated by Shyam Singh Shashi
sources.note: Biographical account of Chennamma's role in the Kittur resistance and its memory in Karnataka.

episodes.notes: The Kittur disturbance and Thackeray's death are documented in colonial gazetteers; Chennamma's speeches and some battlefield details are later biographical reconstruction and should be dramatized cautiously.

episodes.title: Rayanna's Forest Promise
episodes.logline: After Kittur fell, Rayanna is remembered for keeping Chennamma's cause alive through swift forest-side resistance and village support. His story turns defeat into a promise to keep working for freedom.
episodes.moral: Loyalty means continuing the work for justice even after a setback.

sources.citation: Gazetteer of the Bombay Presidency, vol. XXII: Dharwar, Government Central Press, 1884, historical section on Kittur and subsequent administration
sources.note: Provides the documented Kittur context in which Rayanna's later resistance is situated.

sources.citation: Suryanath U. Kamath, A Concise History of Karnataka, MCC, chapters on the Kittur rising and Sangolli Rayanna
sources.note: Modern Karnataka historical synthesis for Rayanna's guerrilla resistance and execution after the Kittur struggle.

episodes.notes: Rayanna is strongly preserved in Karnataka historical memory and later scholarship; many dramatic details come from folk ballads, so the episode should avoid invented dialogues or unsupported tactical claims.

episodes.title: Tirot Sing and the Road Through the Hills
episodes.logline: When a proposed road through Khasi territory began to feel like a threat to self-rule, Tirot Sing and his people withdrew trust and resisted. The hills became a lesson in listening to local consent before changing a homeland.
episodes.moral: Respect for a community's land and voice is essential to just leadership.

sources.citation: P. R. T. Gurdon, The Khasis, Macmillan, 1907, introduction on David Scott, Nongkhlaw, and the April 1829 attack
sources.note: Documents the road negotiations with the Syiem of Nongkhlaw, the start of construction, and the outbreak of conflict.

sources.citation: Government of India, Ministry of Information, Films Division, Raja of Nongkhlaw, Films Division No. 008927
sources.note: Official synopsis identifies Tirot Sing as Syiem of Nongkhlaw and recounts the resistance and his death in captivity.

episodes.notes: British accounts call the 1829 event a massacre; Khasi memory frames it as resistance to encroachment. The episode should use neutral wording and avoid celebrating harm to individuals.

episodes.title: Uda Devi in the Tree at Sikandar Bagh
episodes.logline: During the storming of Sikandar Bagh, a hidden defender high in a tree slowed the advance and made even opponents pause in astonishment. Later memory in Lucknow names her Uda Devi and honours her daring stand.
episodes.moral: Bravery is not limited by gender, rank, or whether history writes down your name at once.

sources.citation: William Forbes-Mitchell, Reminiscences of the Great Mutiny, 1857-59, Macmillan, 1893, chapter IV, Sikandar/ Secundrabagh episode
sources.note: Eyewitness memoir describes a woman defender in a tree at Sikandar Bagh, armed with pistols, whose courage shocked the soldiers who discovered her.

sources.citation: Badri Narayan, Women Heroes and Dalit Assertion in North India: Culture, Identity and Politics, Sage, 2006, chapter on Uda Devi and Dalit memory
sources.note: Explains how the unnamed woman in colonial memory became identified and commemorated as Uda Devi in Pasi/Dalit public history.

episodes.notes: The woman defender at Sikandar Bagh is documented in Forbes-Mitchell; the name Uda Devi and details of her background are later community memory and should be presented as such.

episodes.title: Jhalkari Bai's Decoy at Jhansi
episodes.logline: As danger closed around Jhansi, Jhalkari Bai is remembered for stepping forward so attention would turn away from the queen. Whether every detail can be proven or not, the story celebrates a helper whose courage protected a larger cause.
episodes.moral: Unsung courage often means helping someone else carry the mission forward.

sources.citation: Lok Sabha Secretariat, 'Need to give due recognition to an associate namely Jhalkari Bai of Maharani Laxmibai...', 22 November 2007
sources.note: Parliamentary record of modern public recognition of Jhalkari Bai as an associate of Lakshmibai in the First War of Independence.

sources.citation: Badri Narayan, Women Heroes and Dalit Assertion in North India: Culture, Identity and Politics, Sage, 2006, discussion of Jhalkari Bai memory
sources.note: Analyses the Dalit/community memory, songs, and public commemoration around Jhalkari Bai.

episodes.notes: The siege of Jhansi is well documented, but the famous impersonation/decoy episode rests mainly on later oral, caste-community, and nationalist memory rather than contemporary British records. It should be labelled as remembered tradition.

episodes.title: Peer Ali Khan's Quiet Resolve
episodes.logline: In Patna, bookseller Peer Ali Khan was accused of helping organize resistance and faced questioning with calm resolve. His remembered words teach that conviction can stay steady even when the future is dark.
episodes.moral: A clear conscience can give strength in the hardest moment.

sources.citation: William Tayler, The Patna Crisis; or, Three Months at Patna during the Insurrection of 1857, 1858, pp. 62-67
sources.note: Contemporary colonial account naming Peer Ali Khan as a leader in Patna, describing papers found at his house and his composure after sentence.

sources.citation: History of the Freedom Movement in Bihar, Government of Bihar/archival edition, chapter on 1857 in Patna
sources.note: Regional synthesis placing Peer Ali Khan within Bihar's 1857 resistance.

episodes.notes: Tayler is hostile and colonial in tone, so use it for event details while avoiding its derogatory labels. Peer Ali's quoted defiance is recorded by his opponent and should be handled as a reported statement.

episodes.title: Tilka Manjhi and the Rajmahal Memory
episodes.region: Rajmahal Hills and Bhagalpur, present-day Jharkhand/Bihar
episodes.logline: In the Rajmahal hills, Tilka Manjhi is remembered as a leader who called forest communities to resist unfair control. The episode presents his courage as a living regional memory while carefully separating legend from record.
episodes.moral: Respecting history means honouring memory while still asking what the records can prove.

sources.citation: W. W. Hunter, The Annals of Rural Bengal, Smith, Elder & Co., 1868, chapter on the hill-men of Rajmahal and Augustus Cleveland
sources.note: Documents the Rajmahal hill context, Paharia/Santal frontier, and Cleveland's policy in the 1780s.

sources.citation: K. S. Singh (ed.), Tribal Movements in India, vol. 1, Manohar, 1982, sections on early tribal resistance in eastern India
sources.note: Scholarly context for early tribal resistance traditions including the memory of Tilka/Jabra in the Rajmahal region.

episodes.notes: The popular story that Tilka Manjhi wounded Augustus Cleveland with an arrow and was later executed is widely commemorated but is difficult to verify in readily available colonial records; this episode should foreground the documented Rajmahal resistance context and mark the arrow story as tradition.

episodes.title: Birsa's Call at Dombari Buru
episodes.logline: At the height of the Ulgulan, Birsa Munda's followers gathered around a promise of land, dignity, and renewal. The hills of Dombari Buru became a symbol of a community demanding to be heard.
episodes.moral: People need both justice for their land and respect for their culture.

sources.citation: K. S. Singh, Birsa Munda and His Movement, 1874-1901, Oxford University Press, 1983, chapters 'The Uprising' and 'The End'
sources.note: Detailed scholarly study using official reports, witness depositions, folklore, and trial materials for the Ulgulan.

sources.citation: Trial and deposition extracts of Munda prisoners, reproduced as appendices in K. S. Singh, Birsa Munda and His Movement, 1874-1901
sources.note: Primary-record extracts supporting the legal aftermath and colonial understanding of the movement.

episodes.notes: Dombari/Sail Rakab memories include powerful folklore; the episode should avoid graphic depiction of firing and focus on the gathering, demands, and legacy.

episodes.title: Gaidinliu's Courage Behind Bars
episodes.region: Zeliangrong areas of Manipur, Nagaland, and Assam
episodes.logline: As a teenager, Gaidinliu carried forward a movement for faith, identity, and self-rule after Jadonang. Even when imprisoned far from home, she remained a sign of courage for the hills.
episodes.moral: Young people can become guardians of culture and freedom with patience and resolve.

sources.citation: Gangmumei Kamei, A History of Modern Manipur, 1826-2000, vol. II, chapter 'The Naga Raj Movement'
sources.note: Discusses the Zeliangrong movement under Jadonang and Gaidinliu and later appeals for her release from Shillong jail.

sources.citation: The Gazette of India, Extraordinary, G.S.R. 33(E), 15 January 2015, Coinage rules for the birth centenary of Rani Gaidinliu
sources.note: Official Government of India commemoration identifying Rani Gaidinliu and her national recognition.

episodes.notes: The movement has religious, cultural, and political dimensions and is interpreted differently by colonial and community sources. Present it as anti-colonial and cultural self-assertion without flattening Naga diversity.

episodes.title: Matangini Hazra Carries the Flag
episodes.logline: At Tamluk, elderly Matangini Hazra walked at the front of a procession with the tricolour held high. She did not return home, but her calm courage helped others remember the flag as a promise of freedom.
episodes.moral: Age is no barrier to standing gently and bravely for what is right.

sources.citation: P. N. Chopra (ed.), Who's Who of Indian Martyrs, vol. 1, Publications Division, Government of India, entry 'Hajra, (Smt.) Matangini'
sources.note: Records her Civil Disobedience work, Quit India participation, leading a Tamluk procession on 29 September 1942, and death while carrying the tricolour.

sources.citation: Bipan Chandra et al., India's Struggle for Independence, chapter on Quit India and parallel governments
sources.note: Provides wider context for the mass character of Quit India, including Bengal's militant local mobilization.

episodes.notes: The Tamluk procession and her death are documented in government martyr references; visuals should show procession, flag, and symbolic light, not injury.

episodes.title: Kanaklata Barua at Gohpur
episodes.logline: Teenager Kanaklata Barua led a group toward the Gohpur police station with the tricolour. Her brave walk became one of Assam's tender, powerful memories of youthful sacrifice.
episodes.moral: The courage of the young can inspire an entire community to hope.

sources.citation: P. N. Chopra (ed.), Who's Who of Indian Martyrs, vol. 1, Publications Division, Government of India, entry 'Barua, (Kumari) Kanaklata'
sources.note: Records her birth at Borangabari, role in Quit India, leading a procession to Gohpur police station, and death on 20 September 1942.

sources.citation: Arun Chandra Bhuyan, Nationalist Upsurge in Assam, Government of Assam/Publication Board Assam, sections on Quit India in Assam
sources.note: Regional historical context for student and youth participation in Assam during 1942.

episodes.notes: The procession and date are documented; keep the child-facing version symbolic and avoid showing gunfire or wounds.

episodes.title: Potti Sreeramulu's Fast for a Language Home
episodes.logline: Potti Sreeramulu undertook a long fast asking that Telugu-speaking people have a state where their language could flourish. His sacrifice stirred the country into a new conversation about language, identity, and democratic belonging.
episodes.moral: Peaceful sacrifice can move a nation when it asks for dignity without hatred.

sources.citation: The Gazette of India, Extraordinary, Issue No. 577, Ministry of Law, 14 September 1953, The Andhra State Act, 1953
sources.note: Primary legal record creating Andhra State after the movement for a Telugu-speaking state.

sources.citation: Report of the States Reorganisation Commission, Government of India, 1955, introductory discussion of Andhra and linguistic reorganisation
sources.note: Explains the national impact of the Andhra demand and the wider linguistic-states question after Sreeramulu's fast.

episodes.notes: His fast and death are central to Andhra statehood memory; avoid depicting physical decline. Frame the episode around peaceful resolve, language dignity, and constitutional change.

episodes.title: Pritilata at Pahartali
episodes.logline: Pritilata Waddedar led a small group against a club that displayed an unfair racial sign, turning quiet hurt into a public call for dignity. The story keeps the focus on her resolve, education, and refusal to accept humiliation.
episodes.moral: Self-respect grows stronger when it stands up for everyone's dignity.

sources.citation: Kalpana Dutt, Chittagong Armoury Raiders: Reminiscences, People's Publishing House, 1945/1979
sources.note: Gives insider context for women revolutionaries in the Chittagong group and the Pahartali action.

sources.citation: Shankar Ghosh, Pritilata Waddedar, National Book Trust, biography in the National Biography series
sources.note: Biographical source for Pritilata's education, revolutionary work, and Pahartali episode.

episodes.notes: The discriminatory club sign and Pahartali action are documented; the episode should avoid showing injury or death and can symbolize the final peril with a lamp going out.

episodes.title: Durga Bhabhi's Train Journey
episodes.logline: After Lahore became dangerous for the young revolutionaries, Durga Bhabhi calmly travelled by train with Bhagat Singh in disguise, helping him slip past watchful eyes. Her courage shows how quiet presence of mind can change history.
episodes.moral: Bravery is not always loud; sometimes it is calm, clever, and protective.

sources.citation: A. G. Noorani, The Trial of Bhagat Singh: Politics of Justice, Oxford University Press, 1996
sources.note: Discusses the HSRA context and Bhagat Singh's escape from Lahore after the Saunders case.

sources.citation: Kama Maclean, Revolutionary History of Interwar India: Violence, Image, Voice and Text, Oxford University Press, 2015
sources.note: Places Durga Devi Vohra and revolutionary memory within interwar nationalist networks.

episodes.notes: The train-escape episode is widely recorded in revolutionary memoir and scholarship; exact conversations are not secure and should not be invented.

episodes.title: Usha Mehta's Secret Radio Voice
episodes.logline: Young Usha Mehta helped run the underground Congress Radio, sending messages of hope when many leaders were in prison. Her hidden microphone shows how truth can travel even through silence and fear.
episodes.moral: A truthful voice, used responsibly, can encourage many people.

sources.citation: Usha Thakkar, Congress Radio: Usha Mehta and the Underground Radio Station of 1942, Penguin/Viking, 2022
sources.note: Detailed study of the station, its operators, broadcasts, and shutdown.

sources.citation: Bombay Home Department and trial records for the Congress Radio case, Crown v. Usha Mehta and others, 1942-43, Maharashtra State Archives
sources.note: Primary case records for arrests and charges related to the underground broadcasts.

episodes.notes: The radio station operated for a short period in 1942 and was traced by authorities; avoid spy-thriller exaggeration beyond documented broadcasting and arrests.

episodes.title: The Murmu Brothers' Bhognadih Call
episodes.figure: Sido Murmu, Kanhu Murmu, Chand Murmu and Bhairav Murmu
episodes.region: Bhognadih and the Santhal Parganas, present-day Jharkhand
episodes.logline: At Bhognadih, Sido, Kanhu, Chand, and Bhairav Murmu became voices for Santhal communities facing heavy pressure from revenue demands and outsiders. Their call began the Hul, remembered as a struggle for dignity, land, and community rights.
episodes.moral: People protect their community best when they stand together with purpose.

sources.citation: L. S. S. O'Malley and R. H. McPherson, Bengal District Gazetteers: Santal Parganas, Bengal Secretariat Book Depot, 1910
sources.note: Gazetteer account of the Santhal Hul, Bhognadih, and the Murmu leaders.

sources.citation: W. W. Hunter, The Annals of Rural Bengal, Smith, Elder & Co., 1868, chapters on the Santal rebellion
sources.note: Early colonial-era synthesis based on official records and testimony; useful but should be read critically.

episodes.notes: The Hul and Murmu leadership are documented; claims of divine commands and miracle details belong to community memory/tradition and should be labelled if used.

episodes.title: Komaram Bheem's Jodeghat Stand
episodes.logline: Komaram Bheem spoke for Gond communities who wanted dignity over their water, forests, and land. At Jodeghat, his stand became a memory of belonging and responsibility to the earth.
episodes.moral: Land, forests, and water are shared trusts that communities must protect with wisdom.

sources.citation: Christoph von Fürer-Haimendorf, The Raj Gonds of Adilabad: A Peasant Culture of the Deccan, Macmillan, 1948
sources.note: Ethnographic and historical context for Gond society in Adilabad soon after Bheem's period.

sources.citation: Government of Telangana, Revenue Department G.O.Ms.No.224, 11 October 2016, naming Kumuram Bheem Asifabad district
sources.note: Official recognition of Komaram/Kumuram Bheem's regional historical importance.

episodes.notes: Bheem's resistance and death at Jodeghat are part of documented regional history; the exact slogan 'Jal, Jangal, Zameen' is strongly associated with him in later memory, but wording should be flagged as remembered tradition unless a primary wording is located.

episodes.title: Parbati Giri Enters the Courtroom
episodes.logline: As a teenager in Odisha, Parbati Giri joined Quit India work with fearless energy and is remembered for walking into official spaces to ask for freedom with dignity. Her story shows that young people can serve bravely without losing kindness.
episodes.moral: Age is no barrier to service when courage is joined with compassion.

sources.citation: Giridhari Prasad Guru, Parbati Giri: The Freedom Fighter, Sambalpur University, 2005
sources.note: Focused biography of Parbati Giri as a nationalist and later social reformer from Odisha.

sources.citation: Harekrushna Mahatab, History of the Freedom Movement in Orissa, vol. 4, Government of Orissa, 1957
sources.note: Provides regional context for Quit India activity in Odisha.

episodes.notes: Her Quit India participation and imprisonments are historically grounded; the precise courtroom wording varies in retellings and should be paraphrased, not quoted.

episodes.title: Pingali Venkayya's Flag Sketch
episodes.logline: Pingali Venkayya studied flag designs and brought ideas to the national movement, helping Indians imagine a shared banner. The episode follows a sketch becoming a symbol that millions could salute together.
episodes.moral: Careful study and creativity can give a nation a symbol of unity.

sources.citation: M. K. Gandhi, 'The National Flag', Young India, 13 April 1921
sources.note: Contemporary nationalist discussion of the proposed flag at the time of the Bezwada Congress.

sources.citation: Constituent Assembly Debates, 22 July 1947, Jawaharlal Nehru's resolution on the national flag
sources.note: Primary debate record for the adopted tricolour and the Ashoka Chakra modification.

episodes.notes: Venkayya's design work is accepted in national memory, but the 1947 adopted flag was a modified national design; avoid saying one person alone designed every final feature.

episodes.title: Vanchinathan at Maniyachi Station
episodes.logline: At Maniyachi railway station, Vanchinathan took a drastic revolutionary path against Collector Robert Ashe, and the moment ended in tragedy. A child-safe retelling can explore why anger needs moral guidance even in a freedom struggle.
episodes.moral: Love for the country must be joined with wisdom, restraint, and care for life.

sources.citation: Sedition Committee Report, 1918, Government of India, sections on revolutionary activity in the Madras Presidency and the Ashe murder
sources.note: Official inquiry summary of the Maniyachi incident and wider revolutionary networks.

sources.citation: Tinnevelly Riots, Conspiracy and Ashe Murder records, Madras Presidency judicial/police compilations, 1911-1912
sources.note: Primary administrative records for the Maniyachi case and related investigation.

episodes.notes: This episode involves political assassination and suicide; for children, show the railway platform, a dropped letter, and sombre consequences without depicting harm.

episodes.title: Garimella's Song Says No
episodes.logline: Garimella Satyanarayana's Telugu song 'Maa Koddi Telladoratanam' turned protest into a tune people could remember. The episode shows how a song can carry courage from village to village.
episodes.moral: Art can give people a peaceful language for saying what is right.

sources.citation: Garimella Satyanarayana, 'Maa Koddi Telladoratanam', nationalist song pamphlet/song text, c. 1921, later collected in Telugu patriotic song anthologies
sources.note: Primary song text associated with Non-Cooperation protest in Andhra.

sources.citation: Sarojini Regani, Highlights of the Freedom Movement in Andhra Pradesh, Government/Andhra historical publication, 1972
sources.note: Regional synthesis for the Non-Cooperation movement and Andhra patriotic culture.

episodes.notes: The song and Garimella's imprisonment are well remembered in Andhra history; exact crowd reactions should be treated as illustrative unless tied to a cited newspaper report.

series.title: Ancient & Medieval India
series.origin: Indian subcontinent, c. 6th c BCE – 17th c CE

episodes.title: Porus at the River Hydaspes
episodes.logline: At a rain-swollen river, Porus faced Alexander with courage and discipline. Greek accounts remember that, after the battle, he asked to be treated 'like a king' and retained his dignity.
episodes.moral: True courage includes dignity in both danger and defeat.

sources.citation: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book V.15-19, Hydaspes campaign
sources.note: Primary Greek narrative for the river crossing, battle, and Porus's royal bearing.

sources.citation: Plutarch, Life of Alexander, ch. 60
sources.note: Preserves the famous exchange about being treated as a king.

sources.citation: A.B. Bosworth, Conquest and Empire: The Reign of Alexander the Great, Hydaspes discussion
sources.note: Modern synthesis weighing the Greek accounts and battlefield context.

episodes.notes: Documented mainly from Greek sources; no contemporary Indian inscription for Porus is known. Later claims that Alexander was decisively defeated by Porus are not supported by the earliest evidence.

episodes.title: Chandragupta and Chanakya Build an Empire
episodes.logline: From the power center of Pataliputra, Chandragupta forged a vast realm and met Seleucus Nicator through diplomacy after conflict. Chanakya's statecraft tradition gives the episode its lesson in planning, discipline, and governance.
episodes.moral: Great victories last when courage is joined to wise administration.

sources.citation: Appian, Syriaca, ch. 55, on Seleucus and Sandrokottos
sources.note: Supports the Seleucus-Chandragupta settlement and diplomatic recognition.

sources.citation: Strabo, Geography XV.1.36 and XV.1.53-56, preserving Megasthenes' Indica
sources.note: Describes Palibothra/Pataliputra and Mauryan-era royal administration known to Megasthenes.

sources.citation: Kautilya, Arthashastra, esp. Books VI-VII
sources.note: Primary statecraft text attributed to Chanakya/Kautilya; supports administrative ideas rather than a scene-by-scene biography.

episodes.notes: Chandragupta's reign and Seleucid treaty are documented in Greco-Roman sources; Chanakya's direct role in the founding story is preserved in later Indian literary traditions and should be presented as tradition-informed, not inscriptional fact.

episodes.title: Ashoka's Kalinga Change of Heart
episodes.logline: After conquering Kalinga, Ashoka had his remorse carved on stone and chose 'conquest by dhamma' as the better path. The episode can show a king looking at a quiet battlefield from afar, then turning toward compassion and public welfare.
episodes.moral: The strongest leaders can admit harm and choose a better way.

sources.citation: Major Rock Edict XIII of Ashoka
sources.note: Primary record for the Kalinga conquest, Ashoka's remorse, and 'conquest by dhamma'.

sources.citation: Separate Kalinga Rock Edicts I-II (Dhauli/Jaugada)
sources.note: Shows Ashoka addressing officials in Kalinga with welfare, justice, and 'all men are my children'.

sources.citation: D.C. Sircar, Inscriptions of Asoka; Romila Thapar, Asoka and the Decline of the Mauryas
sources.note: Scholarly translations and historical framing of the edicts.

episodes.notes: The transformation is unusually well documented because Ashoka states his remorse in his own edicts. Avoid legendary embellishments from later Buddhist texts unless clearly labelled.

episodes.title: Kharavela Opens the Cave Chronicle
episodes.era: Mahameghavahana/Chedi of Kalinga, c. 1st century BCE
episodes.logline: On the wall of Hathigumpha cave, Kharavela's reign unfolds year by year: public works, music, repairs, and bold campaigns. The stone record lets children meet a ruler through the words his court chose to preserve.
episodes.moral: A legacy is built not only by power, but by works that help people remember and thrive.

sources.citation: Hathigumpha Inscription of Kharavela, Udayagiri, lines 1-17
sources.note: Primary source for Kharavela's regnal-year narrative, works, campaigns, and Jain associations.

sources.citation: Shashi Kant, The Hathigumpha Inscription of Kharavela and the Bhabru Edict of Asoka: A Critical Study, 1971
sources.note: Detailed edition, translation, and discussion of disputed readings.

sources.citation: D.C. Sircar, Select Inscriptions Bearing on Indian History and Civilisation, Hathigumpha section
sources.note: Standard epigraphic translation and historical notes.

episodes.notes: The inscription is weathered and some readings, dates, and campaign identifications are debated; present achievements with 'the inscription says' phrasing where needed.

episodes.title: Gautamiputra Restores the Satavahana Standard
episodes.logline: Gautamiputra Satakarni is remembered in cave inscriptions as a ruler who revived Satavahana power in the Deccan. The story can center on a royal dedication at Nashik, where his mother Gautami Balashri preserves his fame in stone.
episodes.moral: Duty to family and country can inspire achievements that outlast a lifetime.

sources.citation: Nashik Cave Inscription of Gautami Balashri, Cave 3, Epigraphia Indica VIII
sources.note: Primary eulogy naming Gautamiputra Satakarni and describing his victories and virtues.

sources.citation: Overstruck Nahapana coins attributed to Gautamiputra Satakarni; E.J. Rapson, Catalogue of the Coins of the Andhra Dynasty
sources.note: Numismatic evidence for Satavahana-Western Kshatrapa conflict and recovery of territory.

sources.citation: R.C. Majumdar, The History and Culture of the Indian People, Vol. II, Satavahana chapters
sources.note: Scholarly synthesis of inscriptional and coin evidence.

episodes.notes: The Nashik inscription is laudatory; claims of destroying Sakas, Yavanas, and Pahlavas should be explained as royal eulogy supported partly by coin evidence.

episodes.title: Samudragupta's Pillar of Many Realms
episodes.logline: Harishena's pillar poem presents Samudragupta as a conqueror, diplomat, patron, and musician. Instead of showing combat, the episode can show envoys, maps, and a veena, revealing many kinds of royal strength.
episodes.moral: Power is richest when joined with learning, art, and wise diplomacy.

sources.citation: Allahabad Pillar Inscription (Prayaga Prashasti) of Samudragupta, composed by Harishena, tr. J.F. Fleet in Corpus Inscriptionum Indicarum, Vol. III
sources.note: Primary source for campaigns, tributary rulers, diplomacy, and royal qualities.

sources.citation: Samudragupta gold coin types, including Lyrist and Ashvamedha types; John Allan, Catalogue of the Coins of the Gupta Dynasties
sources.note: Supports claims of royal ritual, prestige, and musical self-presentation.

sources.citation: R.C. Majumdar, The Classical Age, Gupta chapters
sources.note: Historical synthesis using inscriptions and coinage.

episodes.notes: The prashasti is a court eulogy and must be read as praise poetry; its lists of regions are key evidence but not a neutral battle diary.

episodes.title: Vikramaditya and the Iron Pillar's Riddle
episodes.logline: At Udayagiri, inscriptions place Chandragupta II's minister beside sacred caves and royal ambition. A second clue, the Iron Pillar's 'King Chandra,' invites children to solve history like detectives, with evidence and caution.
episodes.moral: Good history asks for evidence before turning admiration into certainty.

sources.citation: Udayagiri Cave Inscription of Virasena, minister of Chandragupta II, Gupta year 82
sources.note: Primary evidence linking Chandragupta II's court to Udayagiri.

sources.citation: Mehrauli Iron Pillar Inscription of King Chandra, tr. J.F. Fleet/D.C. Sircar
sources.note: Primary inscription often, but not unanimously, identified with Chandragupta II.

sources.citation: Faxian, A Record of Buddhist Kingdoms, travels in Gupta north India
sources.note: Contemporary travel account for conditions in north India during the broad Gupta period.

episodes.notes: The identification of the Iron Pillar's 'Chandra' with Chandragupta II is strong but debated; present it as a leading scholarly view, not absolute fact.

episodes.title: Aryabhata and Brahmagupta Measure the Sky
episodes.logline: Aryabhata described a rotating Earth and elegant calculations; Brahmagupta later gave rules that helped shape arithmetic with zero and negative numbers. The drama is a night sky turning into numbers on a scholar's palm-leaf page.
episodes.moral: Curiosity and careful calculation can change how people understand the universe.

sources.citation: Aryabhata, Aryabhatiya, Gitikapada and Golapada, composed 499 CE
sources.note: Primary mathematical-astronomical text for Aryabhata's methods and cosmological statements.

sources.citation: Brahmagupta, Brahmasphutasiddhanta, ch. 18
sources.note: Primary source for arithmetic rules involving zero and negative quantities.

sources.citation: Kim Plofker, Mathematics in India, chapters on Aryabhata and Brahmagupta
sources.note: Modern scholarly explanation of the technical claims and their limits.

episodes.notes: This is a science/culture achievement episode, not a court anecdote. Avoid claiming modern discoveries too broadly; describe the specific rules and models found in the texts.

episodes.title: Xuanzang Reaches Nalanda
episodes.era: Early medieval learning, c. 7th century CE
episodes.logline: After a long journey, Xuanzang studied at Nalanda, a great mahavihara filled with teachers, debates, and travelers from many lands. The episode can follow a young learner entering a brick campus where questions are treasures.
episodes.moral: Learning grows when people travel, listen, debate, and share knowledge respectfully.

sources.citation: Xuanzang, Great Tang Records on the Western Regions, account of Nalanda
sources.note: Primary travel account for Nalanda's teachers, students, curriculum, and reputation.

sources.citation: Yijing, A Record of the Buddhist Religion as Practised in India and the Malay Archipelago
sources.note: Primary monastic travel account describing Indian Buddhist learning networks.

sources.citation: ASI/UNESCO, Archaeological Site of Nalanda Mahavihara World Heritage dossier
sources.note: Archaeological evidence for the large monastic-scholastic complex and its long occupation.

episodes.notes: Nalanda's scale and fame are documented by travel accounts and archaeology. Specific dramatic details such as entrance examinations should be used only if tied to the travel texts, not as generic legend.

episodes.title: Pulakeshin II Checks Harsha's March
episodes.logline: The Aihole inscription celebrates Pulakeshin II as the Deccan ruler before whom Harsha's northern advance stopped. The child-safe scene can show two great powers facing each other across a river frontier, with banners lowered in respect.
episodes.moral: Defending one's home can require courage, preparation, and respect for a worthy rival.

sources.citation: Aihole Inscription of Pulakeshin II, composed by Ravikirti, Saka 556/634 CE
sources.note: Primary Chalukya source for Pulakeshin's reign and the claim of checking Harsha.

sources.citation: Xuanzang, Great Tang Records, account of Mo-ho-la-cha/Maharashtra and its ruler
sources.note: External travel account noting the Deccan ruler whom Harsha could not subdue.

sources.citation: D.C. Sircar, Select Inscriptions; R.C. Majumdar, The Classical Age
sources.note: Scholarly translations and context for Aihole and Chalukya-Gupta/Harsha politics.

episodes.notes: Aihole is royal praise, while Xuanzang provides external support. The exact battlefield details are not preserved; keep visuals symbolic.

episodes.title: Mamalla's Stones by the Sea
episodes.logline: Narasimhavarman I, called Mamalla, gave his name to a port of carved rocks, rathas, caves, and ocean-side art. His age also remembered Pallava success at Vatapi, turning strength into monuments that still teach beauty.
episodes.moral: The finest victories are those that leave learning, art, and wonder behind.

sources.citation: Kuram Copper-Plate Grant of Paramesvaravarman I, Pallava records referring to Narasimhavarman's Vatapi victory
sources.note: Dynastic epigraphic evidence for Pallava-Chalukya conflict and Narasimhavarman's prestige.

sources.citation: UNESCO/ASI, Group of Monuments at Mahabalipuram World Heritage description
sources.note: Archaeological and art-historical evidence for Mamalla-period rock-cut caves, rathas, and reliefs.

sources.citation: K.A. Nilakanta Sastri, A History of South India, Pallava chapters
sources.note: Scholarly synthesis of Pallava inscriptions and monument chronology.

episodes.notes: Attribution of individual monuments within Mamallapuram can be complex across Pallava reigns; avoid claiming every monument was personally built by Narasimhavarman I.

episodes.title: The Chola Village Chooses Its Guardians
episodes.era: Chola administration under Parantaka I, c. 919-921 CE
episodes.logline: At Uttaramerur, village rules were carved in stone: who could serve on committees, who was disqualified, and how names were chosen by the pot-ticket method. This is an episode about fairness, responsibility, and public trust.
episodes.moral: A strong community needs clear rules and honest service.

sources.citation: Uttaramerur Vaikunta Perumal Temple inscription of Parantaka I, year 12, kudavolai committee rules
sources.note: Primary source for qualifications, disqualifications, and committee selection procedure.

sources.citation: Uttaramerur inscription of Parantaka I, year 14, supplementary village assembly regulations
sources.note: Primary continuation/refinement of local governance rules.

sources.citation: K.A. Nilakanta Sastri, The Cholas; South Indian Inscriptions editions by E. Hultzsch/H. Krishna Sastri
sources.note: Scholarly translation and administrative context.

episodes.notes: This was not modern universal democracy; it was a structured village assembly with property, learning, age, and conduct qualifications. Present it accurately as local self-governance.
episodes.chapter_part: I. Rise of the Tiger Throne

episodes.title: Rajaraja Raises Brihadisvara
episodes.logline: Rajaraja Chola I turned royal power into a towering temple, carefully recording gifts, workers, lamps, music, and administration on stone. The episode can show planners, artisans, and scribes building a monument together.
episodes.moral: Great works endure when vision is matched with organization and respect for skill.

sources.citation: Thanjavur Rajarajesvara/Brihadisvara Temple inscriptions, ed. E. Hultzsch and V. Venkayya, South Indian Inscriptions
sources.note: Primary records of Rajaraja's endowments, personnel, and temple administration.

sources.citation: UNESCO/ASI, Great Living Chola Temples: Brihadisvara Temple at Thanjavur
sources.note: Archaeological and architectural evidence for construction, consecration, and Chola art.

sources.citation: K.A. Nilakanta Sastri, The Cholas, Rajaraja I chapters
sources.note: Scholarly synthesis from inscriptions and Chola history.

episodes.notes: The temple is also known as Rajarajesvara in inscriptions and Brihadisvara today. Avoid anachronistic claims about construction methods not evidenced by archaeology or inscriptions.

episodes.title: Rajendra Brings the Ganges South
episodes.region: Gangaikonda Cholapuram, Bay of Bengal, and Southeast Asian sea routes
episodes.logline: Rajendra Chola I celebrated northern and overseas campaigns by founding Gangaikonda Cholapuram, 'the city of the Chola who took the Ganges.' The child-safe story can focus on envoys, ships, maps, and a ceremonial pot of river water.
episodes.moral: Ambition should be remembered through purposeful building and capable organization.

sources.citation: Tiruvalangadu Copper Plates of Rajendra Chola I
sources.note: Primary dynastic source for Chola lineage and Rajendra's northern campaign claims.

sources.citation: Tirumalai inscription/meikeerthi of Rajendra Chola I, listing Ganges and Kadaram-related achievements
sources.note: Primary inscriptional basis for the Ganges title and maritime campaign memory.

sources.citation: UNESCO/ASI, Great Living Chola Temples: Brihadisvara at Gangaikondacholapuram
sources.note: Material evidence for Rajendra's capital-temple complex and Chola architecture.

episodes.notes: Campaign lists are royal eulogies; the broad northern expedition and Kadaram campaign are inscriptionally attested, while route details require scholarly caution.

episodes.title: Prithviraj at Tarain: Fact and Ballad
episodes.logline: Prithviraj Chauhan's memory stands at the meeting place of court poem, Persian chronicle, and later bardic legend. The episode follows the documented Tarain struggle while teaching children how to separate source from story.
episodes.moral: Honouring heroes truthfully is stronger than repeating every legend as fact.

sources.citation: Jayanaka, Prithviraja Vijaya, near-contemporary Sanskrit court poem
sources.note: Primary/near-contemporary source for Prithviraj's courtly image and early victories.

sources.citation: Hasan Nizami, Taj-ul-Ma'asir; Minhaj-i-Siraj, Tabaqat-i Nasiri
sources.note: Persian chronicles for the Ghurid-Chahamana conflict and Tarain aftermath.

sources.citation: R.B. Singh, History of the Chahamanas; Cynthia Talbot, The Last Hindu Emperor
sources.note: Modern analysis of source layers and later heroic memory.

episodes.notes: Prithviraj Raso is important bardic tradition but late and layered; dramatic motifs such as the blind archery revenge must be labelled legend, not documented fact.

episodes.title: Krishnadevaraya and the Raichur Resolve
episodes.logline: Portuguese visitor Domingo Paes saw Krishnadevaraya's court at its height and heard of the great Raichur campaign. The episode can show the king reviewing supplies, honoring allies, and returning to a splendid but disciplined capital.
episodes.moral: Confidence becomes greatness when supported by preparation, generosity, and discipline.

sources.citation: Domingo Paes, account in Robert Sewell, A Forgotten Empire: Vijayanagar
sources.note: Near-contemporary eyewitness description of Krishnadevaraya's court and military power.

sources.citation: Fernao Nuniz, chronicle in Sewell, A Forgotten Empire: Vijayanagar
sources.note: Portuguese narrative preserving Vijayanagara traditions and Raichur campaign memory.

sources.citation: Krishnadevaraya inscriptions at Hampi/Krishna temple, Saka 1435 (1513 CE), after Udayagiri campaign
sources.note: Epigraphic evidence for royal victories, temple patronage, and public commemoration.

episodes.notes: Paes is invaluable but still an outside observer; use him for witnessed court detail and compare campaign claims with inscriptions where possible.

episodes.title: Maharana Pratap Keeps the Hills Free
episodes.era: Mewar-Mughal conflict, Haldighati 1576 CE and after
episodes.logline: At Haldighati, Maharana Pratap faced a powerful imperial army and then continued the struggle from the Aravalli hills. The focus is not on gore, but on resilience, mobile leadership, and rebuilding hope.
episodes.moral: Perseverance means protecting one's purpose even after a hard day.

sources.citation: Abu'l Fazl, Akbarnama, Book III, account of the Mewar campaign and Haldighati
sources.note: Primary Mughal court source for the imperial campaign.

sources.citation: Abd al-Qadir Badauni, Muntakhab-ut-Tawarikh, Vol. II, Haldighati account
sources.note: Contemporary chronicle with a different perspective on the campaign.

sources.citation: G.N. Sharma, Mewar and the Mughal Emperors; R.C. Majumdar, The Mughal Empire
sources.note: Modern synthesis on outcome, continued resistance, and later recovery.

episodes.notes: The tactical outcome of Haldighati and later memory are often debated; documented fact supports Pratap's continued resistance and recovery of parts of Mewar, not a simple final victory at Haldighati.

episodes.title: Shivaji's Quiet Escape from Agra
episodes.logline: Held under watch at Agra, Shivaji used patience, secrecy, and timing to return safely to the Deccan. The episode can be a suspenseful escape of disguises, messages, and night travel without showing violence.
episodes.moral: Courage is sometimes the calm intelligence to wait, plan, and act at the right moment.

sources.citation: Krishnaji Anant Sabhasad, Sabhasad Bakhar, tr. S.N. Sen as Siva Chhatrapati
sources.note: Early Maratha narrative for Shivaji's life, Agra episode, and Swaraj memory.

sources.citation: Jedhe Shakavali, entries for Shivaji's Agra visit and return
sources.note: Maratha chronological record supporting dates and sequence.

sources.citation: Jadunath Sarkar, Shivaji and His Times, Agra chapters
sources.note: Critical comparison of Maratha and Persian sources.

episodes.notes: The escape is documented, but details such as baskets/sweets and disguises vary by source tradition; label colorful details as tradition unless tied to a cited text.

episodes.title: Lachit Barphukan Holds the Brahmaputra
episodes.era: Ahom kingdom, Battle of Saraighat 1671 CE
episodes.logline: On the Brahmaputra, Lachit Barphukan helped the Ahom forces use river skill, local knowledge, and resolve at Saraighat. The scene can show boats, mist, drums, and a commander rallying defenders without graphic battle imagery.
episodes.moral: Knowing one's land and working together can overcome a stronger-looking challenge.

sources.citation: Ahom Buranji, tr. G.C. Barua, Saraighat/Lachit traditions
sources.note: Assamese chronicle tradition for Ahom leadership and the river campaign.

sources.citation: Padshah Buranji, ed. S.K. Bhuyan
sources.note: Buranji material on Mughal-Ahom relations and conflict chronology.

sources.citation: S.K. Bhuyan, Lachit Barphukan and His Times
sources.note: Scholarly synthesis from Buranjis and Persian/Assamese records.

episodes.notes: Buranjis are essential regional chronicles but survive in copied/edited forms; avoid modern nationalist embellishments and keep the focus on documented river strategy and leadership.

episodes.title: Rani Durgavati Guards Gondwana
episodes.logline: When a large imperial force entered Gondwana, Rani Durgavati chose to protect her people and command from the front. Her story can show a queen rallying soldiers beside forests and hills, with courage but without graphic battle scenes.
episodes.moral: Duty means standing firm for the people who trust you.

sources.citation: Abu'l-Fazl, Akbarnama, Vol. II, trans. H. Beveridge, account of Asaf Khan's expedition against Garha-Katanga, 1564
sources.note: Mughal court narrative for the campaign, Rani Durgavati's leadership, and Garha-Katanga's wealth and geography.

sources.citation: Nizamuddin Ahmad, Tabaqat-i-Akbari, trans. B. De, section on Akbar's 1564 Garha-Katanga campaign
sources.note: Independent Persian chronicle confirming the campaign and the queen's resistance.

sources.citation: R.V. Russell, The Tribes and Castes of the Central Provinces of India, Vol. III, Gond historical introduction
sources.note: Colonial-era synthesis of Gond polity and later memory, useful only after checking primary chronicles.

episodes.notes: Documented chiefly through Mughal chronicles, which are written from the victor's court perspective. Local heroic memory should be presented respectfully but separated from details not found in early sources.

episodes.title: Rana Kumbha Raises the Fortress of Kumbhalgarh
episodes.logline: Rana Kumbha strengthened Mewar with mountain forts and monuments, including Kumbhalgarh and the Vijay Stambha at Chittor. The episode can focus on builders, guards, and musicians working together to make a realm resilient.
episodes.moral: Strength is not only won in battles; it is built in stone, art, and preparation.

sources.citation: Kumbhalgarh inscription/prashasti of Maharana Kumbha, V.S. 1517 / 1460 CE, published in Epigraphia Indica, Vol. XXIV
sources.note: Primary inscriptional basis for Kumbha's genealogy, titles, and Mewar building-patronage claims.

sources.citation: Ranakpur inscription of V.S. 1496 / 1439 CE, edited by G.H. Ojha, on Kumbha's reign and patronage
sources.note: Contemporary epigraphic evidence for Kumbha's authority and religious-cultural patronage.

sources.citation: Dasharatha Sharma, Rajasthan Through the Ages, Vol. I, chapters on 15th-century Mewar
sources.note: Modern historical synthesis for context around fortification, politics, and culture.

episodes.notes: Kumbha's patronage is inscriptionally grounded. Later bardic accounts about numbers of forts and dramatic episodes should be treated as tradition unless tied to epigraphy or architecture.

episodes.title: Amoghavarsha's Court Gives Kannada a Classic Voice
episodes.figure: Amoghavarsha I Nrupatunga and the Rashtrakutas
