episodes.logline: In the Rashtrakuta age, Amoghavarsha's court helped shape Kavirajamarga, one of the earliest surviving works on Kannada poetics. The story can show a ruler valuing language, learning, and calm statecraft as part of imperial strength.
episodes.moral: A kingdom's glory grows when it protects its language and scholars.

sources.citation: Kavirajamarga, attributed to Nrupatunga Amoghavarsha, 9th century, esp. opening verses on Kannada literary regions
sources.note: Primary literary source for the courtly cultivation of Kannada poetics.

sources.citation: Sanjan copper plates of Amoghavarsha I, Saka 793 / 871 CE, published in Epigraphia Indica, Vol. XVIII
sources.note: Epigraphic evidence for Amoghavarsha's Rashtrakuta rule and titles.

sources.citation: A.S. Altekar, The Rashtrakutas and Their Times, chapters on Amoghavarsha I and Rashtrakuta culture
sources.note: Scholarly synthesis for political setting and literary patronage.

episodes.notes: Kavirajamarga is associated with Amoghavarsha/Nrupatunga, but scholars debate whether he personally authored it or sponsored it. Present the achievement as courtly patronage rather than a guaranteed one-man authorship.

episodes.title: Harsha's Great Assembly of Learning and Giving
episodes.logline: Harsha gathered monks, scholars, envoys, and common people at grand assemblies where debate and generosity were celebrated. A child-safe episode can show banners, books, and gifts, not battlefield rivalry.
episodes.moral: The best rulers make space for learning, dialogue, and care for others.

sources.citation: Banabhatta, Harshacharita, trans. E.B. Cowell and F.W. Thomas, chs. VII-VIII
sources.note: Court biography for Harsha's lineage, courtly world, and royal self-presentation.

sources.citation: Xuanzang, Da Tang Xiyu Ji / Si-yu-ki, trans. Samuel Beal, Book V, accounts of Kanauj and Prayaga assemblies
sources.note: Near-contemporary traveller's account describing Harsha's public assemblies and donations.

sources.citation: R.C. Majumdar, The History and Culture of the Indian People, Vol. III: The Classical Age, chapter on Harsha
sources.note: Modern synthesis comparing literary and travel accounts.

episodes.notes: Harshacharita is a court panegyric and must be balanced with Xuanzang's outsider account and modern scholarship. Harsha's conflict with Pulakeshin II is already covered in batch 1, so this episode focuses on assemblies and culture.

episodes.title: Lalitaditya and the Sun Temple of Martand
episodes.logline: Kalhana remembers Lalitaditya as a powerful Kashmir king, and the ruins of Martand still show the scale of his age's architecture. The episode can follow young builders measuring sunlight across the valley as a monument rises.
episodes.moral: Great achievements can outlast kings when they are built with vision and skill.

sources.citation: Kalhana, Rajatarangini, Book IV, trans. M.A. Stein, verses on Lalitaditya Muktapida
sources.note: Main Sanskrit chronicle source for Lalitaditya's reign and remembered conquests.

sources.citation: Alexander Cunningham, Archaeological Survey of India Report, Vol. II, description of the Martand temple ruins
sources.note: Archaeological description supporting the monument's scale and Kashmir architectural setting.

sources.citation: Hermann Goetz, 'The Conquests of Lalitaditya-Muktapida of Kashmir', Artibus Asiae, 1969
sources.note: Scholarly reassessment of Kalhana's claims and the wider historical context.

episodes.notes: Kalhana wrote in the 12th century, several centuries after Lalitaditya; his conquest list may be enlarged by royal memory. The Martand temple is a documented architectural achievement, while some far-flung military claims should be flagged as debated.

episodes.title: Rudrama Devi Rules in Her Own Name
episodes.figure: Rani Rudrama Devi of the Kakatiyas
episodes.logline: Rudrama Devi governed as a sovereign in a world that expected kings, not queens. Inscriptions and a foreign traveller remember a capable ruler whose authority was recognized across her realm.
episodes.moral: Leadership comes from courage, responsibility, and ability—not from expectations about who should lead.

sources.citation: Malkapuram inscription of Rudrama Devi, 1261 CE, published in South Indian Inscriptions / Epigraphia Andhrica collections
sources.note: Epigraphic evidence for Kakatiya genealogy, grants, and Rudrama Devi's royal authority.

sources.citation: Chandupatla inscription, 1289 CE, Nalgonda district, recording events at the end of Rudrama Devi's reign
sources.note: Important inscription for the later phase of Rudrama Devi's rule and conflict with Ambadeva's circle.

sources.citation: Marco Polo, Travels, Book III, ch. on Mutfili/Motupalli region, Yule-Cordier translation
sources.note: Foreign traveller's account of a just queen in the region, commonly identified with Rudrama Devi, though the identification needs a note.

episodes.notes: Rudrama Devi's sovereignty is inscriptionally documented. Marco Polo's queen is widely associated with her, but because the travel account uses regional names, the identification should be presented as probable rather than the sole proof.

episodes.title: Warangal's Stone Gateways Stand for Kakatiya Resilience
episodes.figure: Prataparudra and the Kakatiya city of Warangal
episodes.logline: Warangal's great fort, gateways, and planned sacred spaces show the engineering confidence of the Kakatiya capital. Even when armies pressed the city, its stones remembered a society skilled in building, trade, and administration.
episodes.moral: A city's strength is made by planners, artisans, farmers, and defenders together.

sources.citation: Amir Khusrau, Khaza'in-ul-Futuh, trans. Muhammad Habib, section on Malik Kafur's Warangal campaign of 1310
sources.note: Contemporary Persian account noting Warangal's political importance and the campaign context.

sources.citation: Ziauddin Barani, Tarikh-i-Firoz Shahi, trans. in Elliot and Dowson, Vol. III, account of Ulugh Khan's Warangal campaign, 1323
sources.note: Later 14th-century chronicle evidence for Prataparudra's Warangal and its final Delhi Sultanate confrontation.

sources.citation: Archaeological Survey of India, Warangal Fort protected monument documentation, Telangana
sources.note: Archaeological basis for the fort, gateways, temple remains, and urban layout.

episodes.notes: This episode should avoid vilifying adversaries and should not turn the siege accounts into graphic violence. The documented focus is the Kakatiya capital's architecture and resilience; later local legends around hidden treasures should be excluded unless sourced.

episodes.title: Sukaphaa Crosses the Patkai and Begins the Ahom Story
episodes.figure: Chaolung Sukaphaa and the early Ahom kingdom
episodes.region: Patkai hills, Charaideo, and the Brahmaputra valley, Assam
episodes.logline: Sukaphaa led his followers across mountains into the Brahmaputra valley and built alliances that began the long Ahom story. The episode can show migration, friendship-making, rice fields, and a new capital at Charaideo.
episodes.moral: A lasting home is built by courage, patience, and respect for neighbours.

sources.citation: Ahom Buranji, trans. G.C. Barua, early chapters on Sukaphaa's migration and settlement at Charaideo
sources.note: Court chronicle tradition for Sukaphaa's journey, alliances, and founding memory.

sources.citation: Edward Gait, A History of Assam, 2nd ed., chapters I-II on the Ahom arrival and early state
sources.note: Scholarly synthesis using Buranjis and colonial-era archival comparison.

sources.citation: S.K. Bhuyan, Studies in the History of Assam, essays on Ahom Buranjis and early Ahom polity
sources.note: Context for how Buranji materials should be read critically.

episodes.notes: The broad Ahom founding tradition is preserved in Buranjis compiled and transmitted over time, so precise dates and speeches should be handled cautiously. Batch 1 already covers Lachit Barphukan; this episode is about the much earlier founding.

episodes.title: Chola Bronze Artists Make the Divine Dance
episodes.figure: Chola bronze artisans and temple patrons
episodes.logline: Chola inscriptions list gifts of metal images, lamps, jewels, and temple service, while surviving bronzes show extraordinary artistry. The episode can follow a workshop casting a graceful image for festival processions.
episodes.moral: Art is a form of devotion, teamwork, and technical mastery.

sources.citation: South Indian Inscriptions, Vol. II, Thanjavur Brihadisvara temple inscriptions of Rajaraja I, entries listing metal images and temple endowments
sources.note: Primary evidence for temple bronzes, donations, lamps, and organized temple service.

sources.citation: C. Sivaramamurti, South Indian Bronzes, Lalit Kala Akademi, 1963
sources.note: Art-historical study of bronze styles, iconography, and casting traditions.

sources.citation: Vidya Dehejia, Art of the Imperial Cholas, Columbia University Press, 1990
sources.note: Modern scholarship connecting inscriptions, ritual movement, and Chola visual culture.

episodes.notes: Rajaraja Chola I already has a batch 1 episode; this one should not retell his biography. Keep the focus on artisans, inscriptions, processional art, and the living temple ecosystem.
episodes.chapter_part: IV. Craft, Trade & the Genius of the Age

episodes.title: Tamil Merchant Guilds Sail the Indian Ocean
episodes.figure: Ainnurruvar/Manigramam merchant guilds and Chola-era trade
episodes.era: Chola-era Indian Ocean trade, 11th-13th century CE
episodes.region: Tamil coast, Southeast Asia, and China Sea routes
episodes.logline: Tamil merchant guilds carried cloth, spices, metalwork, and trust across seas from the Coromandel coast to Southeast Asia. Their inscriptions abroad show that Indian Ocean trade was built on ships, agreements, and shared rules.
episodes.moral: Trust and fair dealing can carry a community farther than any single ship.

sources.citation: Lobu Tua Tamil inscription, Barus, Sumatra, Saka 1010 / 1088 CE, recording the Ainnurruvar merchant guild
sources.note: Primary overseas inscription proving Tamil merchant-guild activity in Southeast Asia.

sources.citation: Zhao Rugua, Zhu Fan Zhi, 1225, trans. Hirth and Rockhill, chapter on Chu-lien/Chola trade
sources.note: Chinese trade account describing Chola-region goods and maritime contacts.

sources.citation: K.A. Nilakanta Sastri, The Colas, chapters on overseas relations and commerce
sources.note: Scholarly synthesis connecting inscriptions, merchant guilds, and Chola-era maritime networks.

episodes.notes: This is not a retelling of Rajendra Chola I's campaign from batch 1. Keep it focused on documented merchant networks and cultural exchange rather than conquest.
episodes.chapter_part: IV. Craft, Trade & the Genius of the Age

episodes.title: Ahilyabai Holkar Rebuilds with Compassion
episodes.region: Maheshwar, Malwa, and pilgrimage networks across India
episodes.logline: Ahilyabai Holkar governed from Maheshwar with a reputation for justice, public works, and support for sacred and civic places across India. The episode can show roads, wells, ghats, and careful listening in court.
episodes.moral: Power is noblest when it repairs, protects, and serves.

sources.citation: John Malcolm, A Memoir of Central India, Vol. II, sections on Ahalya Bai Holkar's administration and public character
sources.note: Early 19th-century account preserving administrative reputation and examples of governance.

sources.citation: Stewart Gordon, The Marathas 1600-1818, New Cambridge History of India, chapter on Maratha state formation and Holkar power
sources.note: Modern context for Malwa politics and Ahilyabai's place in Maratha governance.

sources.citation: Madhya Pradesh District Gazetteer: Indore, sections on Maheshwar and Ahilyabai's public works
sources.note: Gazetteer evidence for built heritage, ghats, and regional memory of civic patronage.

episodes.notes: Ahilyabai belongs to the early modern period, but she is included because the seed list extends the app's 'ancient to medieval' spine into regional early-modern heroes. Avoid miracle stories and stick to governance, patronage, and documented public works.

episodes.title: Bajirao's Swift Ride to Delhi
episodes.logline: Bajirao I became famous for rapid cavalry movement and bold planning, especially the 1737 dash toward Delhi. A child-safe telling can follow maps, messengers, and disciplined riders who outpaced expectations.
episodes.moral: Speed matters most when it is guided by clear thinking and discipline.

sources.citation: Jadunath Sarkar, Fall of the Mughal Empire, Vol. I, chapters on Bajirao's 1737 northern campaign
sources.note: Detailed military-political reconstruction using Persian and Maratha records.

sources.citation: G.S. Sardesai, New History of the Marathas, Vol. II: The Expansion of the Maratha Power, chapters on Bajirao I
sources.note: Maratha-focused synthesis of Bajirao's campaigns, diplomacy, and reputation.

sources.citation: Selections from the Peshwa Daftar, ed. G.S. Sardesai, letters and orders relating to the 1736-1737 northern expedition
sources.note: Administrative correspondence base for movements, planning, and campaign chronology.

episodes.notes: Popular claims that Bajirao was 'undefeated' are common but should be phrased carefully: his career is remembered for remarkable battlefield success and mobility, while each campaign still needs source-specific treatment.

episodes.title: Hampi Amazes the Travellers
episodes.logline: Foreign visitors described Vijayanagara as a vast, wealthy, carefully planned capital, and Hampi's ruins still show markets, temples, waterworks, and royal spaces. The episode can be a guided walk through a buzzing city of many skills.
episodes.moral: A great city is made by planners, farmers, artists, traders, and protectors working together.

sources.citation: Abdur Razzaq Samarqandi, Matla-us-Sadain, 1440s Vijayanagara account, trans. in India in the Fifteenth Century, Hakluyt Society
sources.note: Primary traveller's description of the capital's scale, wealth, and courtly spectacle.

sources.citation: Domingo Paes, account of Vijayanagara c. 1520, in Robert Sewell, A Forgotten Empire: Vijayanagar
sources.note: Eyewitness Portuguese account of streets, markets, waterworks, and royal ceremonies.

sources.citation: UNESCO World Heritage Centre, Group of Monuments at Hampi, Brief Synthesis and criteria
sources.note: Archaeological and heritage summary of Hampi's urban, sacred, hydraulic, and defensive remains.

episodes.notes: Krishnadevaraya and Raichur are already covered in batch 1; this episode is about the capital as an urban achievement. Traveller accounts praise the city but should be balanced with archaeology.

episodes.title: Sushruta Trains the Careful Surgeon
episodes.figure: Sushruta and the Sushruta Samhita tradition
episodes.era: Ancient Indian medical tradition, text layered c. 1st millennium BCE-early CE
episodes.region: North India, later pan-Indian medical learning
episodes.logline: The Sushruta Samhita teaches that students should practise delicate cuts on fruits, gourds, and other safe models before treating people. The episode can celebrate careful training, cleanliness, and compassion in healing.
episodes.moral: Skill should be learned patiently before it is used to help others.

sources.citation: Sushruta Samhita, Sutrasthana 9 (Yogyasutriya Adhyaya), trans. K.L. Bhishagratna, Vol. I
sources.note: Primary medical text section on practical training models for surgical students.

sources.citation: Sushruta Samhita, Sutrasthana 7-8, trans. K.L. Bhishagratna, sections on instruments and surgical preparation
sources.note: Primary source for organized surgical knowledge and instrument classification.

sources.citation: Dominik Wujastyk, The Roots of Ayurveda, selections on the Sushruta Samhita and classical Indian medicine
sources.note: Modern philological context for dating, layers, and medical significance.

episodes.notes: The text is layered and not a modern surgical manual; present its historical significance without claiming uninterrupted modern equivalence. Avoid graphic medical imagery for children.

episodes.title: Nagarjuna's Middle Path of Careful Thinking
episodes.era: Early centuries CE, commonly placed c. 2nd-3rd century CE
episodes.region: Deccan/North India traditions; Buddhist scholastic world
episodes.logline: Nagarjuna taught students to examine ideas carefully and avoid extremes, shaping the Madhyamaka school of thought. The episode can show a calm debate where wisdom means asking better questions.
episodes.moral: Deep thinking begins when we question our own assumptions with humility.

sources.citation: Nagarjuna, Mulamadhyamakakarika, esp. 24.18 on dependent arising and emptiness
sources.note: Primary philosophical text expressing the central Madhyamaka insight.

sources.citation: Kumarajiva's Chinese translation of Nagarjuna's Madhyamaka text tradition, Taisho Tripitaka no. 1564, early 5th century CE
sources.note: Early textual transmission evidence for Nagarjuna's influence beyond India.

sources.citation: David Seyfort Ruegg, The Literature of the Madhyamaka School of Philosophy in India, Harrassowitz, 1981
sources.note: Scholarly guide to sources, attribution problems, and Madhyamaka development.

episodes.notes: Do not conflate the philosopher Nagarjuna with later alchemical or tantric authors named Nagarjuna. This episode is documented as intellectual history, not as a miracle biography.

episodes.title: Muziris Opens the Sangam-Age Sea Road
episodes.figure: Chera/Tamilakam ports and Indian Ocean traders
episodes.era: Sangam age and early historic trade, c. 1st century BCE-3rd century CE
episodes.logline: At Muziris, ships from across the sea met Tamilakam's pepper, pearls, textiles, and poems of bustling ports. The episode can show dockworkers, scribes, and merchants exchanging goods and languages peacefully.
episodes.moral: Openness, trust, and skill turn a port into a bridge between peoples.

sources.citation: Periplus of the Erythraean Sea, sections 54-56, trans. J.W. McCrindle, on Muziris, Nelcynda, and pepper trade
sources.note: Near-contemporary Greco-Roman trade manual naming Muziris and western-coast commerce.

sources.citation: Pliny the Elder, Natural History, Book VI.26, on Roman shipping and Muziris-region trade
sources.note: Roman testimony for long-distance trade with the southwest Indian coast.

sources.citation: P.J. Cherian et al., Pattanam excavations reports on early historic Indian Ocean trade material from Kerala
sources.note: Archaeological context for the Muziris/Pattanam debate and maritime material culture.

episodes.notes: The exact identification of ancient Muziris with Pattanam is influential but still debated. Present the trade network as documented, and the precise site-location question as an active scholarly discussion.

episodes.title: Lokamahadevi's Virupaksha Temple at Pattadakal
episodes.figure: Queen Lokamahadevi and the Early Chalukya builders
episodes.logline: Queen Lokamahadevi sponsored the Virupaksha temple at Pattadakal, where northern and southern architectural ideas met in stone. The episode can show architects comparing plans and carving a shared masterpiece.
episodes.moral: Beautiful things happen when different skills and traditions work together.

sources.citation: Pattadakal Virupaksha temple inscriptions of Lokamahadevi, c. 740 CE, recording the temple foundation
sources.note: Primary epigraphic evidence that Queen Lokamahadevi sponsored the temple.

sources.citation: UNESCO World Heritage Centre, Group of Monuments at Pattadakal, Brief Synthesis, noting Virupaksha temple built by Queen Lokamahadevi in 740 CE
sources.note: Heritage and archaeological summary of the monument's architectural significance.

sources.citation: George Michell, Pattadakal, Oxford University Press / Archaeological Survey of India guide
sources.note: Scholarly architectural analysis of Pattadakal's temple forms and patronage.

episodes.notes: Pulakeshin II is already covered in batch 1; this episode focuses on later Chalukya temple-building and a queen's patronage. The 'victory over Kanchi' context should be stated without triumphalist hostility.

episodes.title: Martanda Varma Wins at Colachel and Builds Travancore
episodes.logline: At Colachel, Travancore defeated a Dutch East India Company force, and Marthanda Varma later used disciplined state-building to strengthen his kingdom. The episode can show strategy, captured cannons, and former foes becoming trainers rather than enemies.
episodes.moral: A wise victory turns danger into learning and stronger institutions.

sources.citation: P. Shungunny Menon, A History of Travancore from the Earliest Times, chapters on Marthanda Varma and Colachel
sources.note: Early historical account of the battle, De Lannoy tradition, and Travancore consolidation.

sources.citation: V. Nagam Aiya, The Travancore State Manual, Vol. I, sections on Marthanda Varma and the Battle of Colachel
sources.note: State-manual synthesis of Travancore records and administrative developments.

sources.citation: A. Galletti, The Dutch in Malabar: Selections from the Records of the Madras Government, 1911, Dutch records around the Travancore conflict
sources.note: Record-based context from Dutch/colonial archival material for the European-company side.

episodes.notes: This is early modern, but included because the seed list extends through regional resistance to European companies. Treat Dutch opponents with dignity and emphasize statecraft, reform, and restraint after victory.

episodes.title: Taming the Kaveri: The Grand Anicut
episodes.era: Early Chola root, c. 2nd century CE
episodes.logline: Long before the imperial Cholas, Karikala throws a great stone dam across the flooding Kaveri, the Grand Anicut, turning a wild river into canals and rice-plains that would one day feed an empire.
episodes.moral: The mightiest empires rest first on water, soil and patient engineering.

sources.citation: The Kallanai (Grand Anicut) across the Kaveri, among the oldest water-diversion structures still in use
Sangam poem Pattinappalai on Karikala Chola; K. A. Nilakanta Sastri, The CōĻas

episodes.chapter_part: I. Rise of the Tiger Throne

episodes.title: Vijayalaya Seizes Thanjavur
episodes.era: Rise of the Imperial Cholas, c. 850 CE
episodes.logline: A chieftain under the Pallavas, Vijayalaya seizes Thanjavur from the Muttaraiyar and raises the tiger banner, founding a line that would one day rule an ocean.
episodes.moral: Great empires begin with one bold, patient beginning.

sources.citation: K. A. Nilakanta Sastri, The CōĻas, 'The Rise of the Cholas'
Tiruvalangadu copper-plate grant (genealogy of the Chola line)

episodes.chapter_part: I. Rise of the Tiger Throne

episodes.title: The Shadow of the Great Vimana
episodes.logline: To crown Rajaraja's temple, the sthapatis raise a sixty-metre tower of interlocking granite and lift an eighty-tonne capstone up a mile-long earthen ramp, an engineering marvel that has stood a thousand years.
episodes.moral: Genius is patience, mathematics, and a thousand hands moving as one.

sources.citation: Engineering studies of the Brihadisvara (Rajarajesvara) vimana and its capstone
George Michell, Architecture and Art of Southern India; the temple's own craftsman inscriptions

episodes.title: Rajaraja's Sword: Kandalur to Lanka
episodes.region: South India, Kerala and Sri Lanka
episodes.logline: Rajaraja's armies and new-built navy break the Cheras at Kandalur Salai, humble the Pandyas, and cross the strait to take Anuradhapura in Lanka, forging a true empire.
episodes.moral: Strength is worthy only when it builds order, not just victory.

sources.citation: K. A. Nilakanta Sastri, The CōĻas, ch. on Rajaraja I
Rajaraja's meykkirti inscriptions (Kandalur Salai, Ilam/Lanka, the Pandya war)

episodes.title: Kundavai, the Chola Princess
episodes.logline: Honoured above queens, Rajaraja's elder sister Kundavai endows temples and hospitals and nurtures the arts, the steady, gracious power beside the tiger throne.
episodes.moral: Power used to heal and build outlasts power used only to conquer.

sources.citation: Chola inscriptions recording Kundavai's endowments (temples and the atula-salai hospital)
K. A. Nilakanta Sastri, The CōĻas

episodes.title: Rajendra's Fleet to Srivijaya
episodes.era: Imperial Chola naval expedition, c. 1025 CE
episodes.region: Bay of Bengal, Srivijaya (Sumatra) and Kadaram (Malaya)
episodes.logline: Rajendra launches the greatest fleet India had ever sent across the eastern sea, striking Srivijaya and Kadaram, the one Indian emperor to carry conquest overseas.
episodes.moral: Vision dares where others dream, yet power reached far must be held with wisdom.

sources.citation: Rajendra's Tanjore inscription listing the Southeast Asian ports (Kadaram, Sri Vijaya)
George Coedes, The Indianized States of Southeast Asia

episodes.title: Kulottunga, the King Who Abolished Tolls
episodes.region: The Chola empire, Kaveri to the Godavari
episodes.logline: Uniting Chola and Eastern Chalukya blood, Kulottunga wins a name no war could buy, 'Sungam Tavirtta', the king who abolished tolls, easing the burden on his people.
episodes.moral: A ruler is remembered less for what he takes than for what he lifts from his people.

sources.citation: Inscriptions granting Kulottunga I the title 'Sungam Tavirtta Cholan' (abolisher of tolls)
K. A. Nilakanta Sastri, The CōĻas, ch. on Kulottunga I

episodes.title: Kamban Sings the Tamil Ramayana
episodes.era: Later Chola cultural age, c. 12th century CE
episodes.logline: Under Chola patronage the poet Kamban composes the Ramavataram, giving Tamil its own soaring Ramayana, proof that an empire's truest monuments can be its words.
episodes.moral: Long after thrones fall, a people's stories keep them alive.

sources.citation: Kamba Ramayanam (Ramavataram) of Kambar
Studies of Tamil literature under the imperial Cholas

episodes.title: Chandragupta I and the Licchavi Bride
episodes.era: Founding of the Gupta empire, c. 319-335 CE
episodes.region: Magadha and the eastern Gangetic plain, Pataliputra
episodes.logline: A Magadha king weds the Licchavi princess Kumaradevi and strikes a gold coin bearing both their names, turning a marriage alliance into the seed of an empire and the start of the Gupta era.
episodes.moral: The strongest thrones rest on alliance and legitimacy, not conquest alone.

sources.citation: Prayag Prashasti (Allahabad Pillar) of Samudragupta, composed by Harishena
sources.note: Retrospective source naming Chandragupta I and the Licchavi connection.

sources.citation: The Chandragupta I-Kumaradevi 'King and Queen' gold coins, legend 'Lichchhavayah', Gupta Brahmi
sources.note: Contemporary coinage naming the queen jointly with the king - unique political propaganda.

sources.citation: Upinder Singh, A History of Ancient and Early Medieval India (2008); R.C. Majumdar (ed.), The Gupta Empire (1946)
sources.note: Chronology and the downsized view of his actual territory.

episodes.notes: He was the first Gupta to claim Maharajadhiraja; his forebears Sri Gupta and Ghatotkaca were only local Maharajas. RECENT SCHOLARSHIP (Upinder Singh; Romila Thapar) DOWNSIZES his realm to Magadha, Prayaga and parts of Bihar - the vast empire is his son Samudragupta's work, so do NOT depict him ruling all India. The Gupta-era epoch (319/320 CE) is conventional and its exact founding is debated. The king-and-queen coin naming Kumaradevi is the earliest Indian case of a queen named on coinage as co-legitimizer - a strong visual anchor. Spoken name should say 'the First'.
episodes.chapter_part: I. The Rise of the Guptas

episodes.title: Samudragupta's Hundred Victories
episodes.region: From the Gangetic plain across the Deccan; the pillar at Prayaga (Allahabad)
episodes.logline: Court poet Harishena carves Samudragupta's conquests onto Ashoka's old pillar: nine northern kings uprooted, twelve southern kings captured and freed, a horse-sacrifice revived, and a warrior-king who has himself struck in gold playing the veena.
episodes.moral: True greatness is measured not only in battles won but in the culture a ruler nurtures.

sources.citation: Prayag Prashasti (Allahabad Pillar) of Harishena, in situ at Allahabad Fort; J.F. Fleet, Corpus Inscriptionum Indicarum Vol. III (1888)
sources.note: The sole primary source for Samudragupta's campaigns.

sources.citation: Samudragupta's gold coins: Archer, Lyrist (veena), Battle-Axe, Tiger-Slayer and Ashvamedha types
sources.note: Contemporary coinage; the Lyrist type shows him as poet-king (Kaviraja).

sources.citation: Daud Ali, Courtly Culture and Political Life in Early Medieval India (2004); Upinder Singh (2008)
sources.note: Reads the Prashasti as royal ideology; critiques the 'Napoleon of India' label.

episodes.notes: TWO DIFFERENT LOGICS: the northern (Aryavarta) kings were ANNEXED; the southern (Dakshinapatha) kings were captured then RE-INSTATED as tributaries (graha-moksha-anugraha), not annexed. He revived the Vedic Ashvamedha (first since the Kanvas) and struck a coin of himself playing the veena. RECENT SCHOLARSHIP rejects V.A. Smith's 'Napoleon of India' (1904) as Eurocentric - his was a mandala/concentric-sovereignty polity, not a European bureaucratic empire. His own poems do not survive (legendary). The pillar is a eulogy, not a survey.
episodes.chapter_part: I. The Rise of the Guptas

episodes.title: Chandragupta II and the Fall of the Shakas
episodes.era: Gupta zenith, c. 375-415 CE (Shaka conquest c. 409 CE)
episodes.region: Malwa, Gujarat and Saurashtra; the Udayagiri caves near Vidisha
episodes.logline: Vikramaditya ends three centuries of foreign Shaka rule in western India, wins the sea-ports, and has the earth-lifting Boar of Vishnu carved at Udayagiri - the god's power mirrored in the king's.
episodes.moral: A ruler's grandest monument is the order and freedom he leaves his people.

sources.citation: Udayagiri Cave 6 inscription of Chandragupta II, Gupta Era 82 (c. 401 CE)
sources.note: The only monument where he is directly named; edited by Michael Willis.

sources.citation: First Gupta silver coinage (rupaka), modeled on Western Kshatrapa coins after the conquest; COININDIA
sources.note: Numismatic proof of the Shaka absorption (~409 CE).

sources.citation: Michael Willis, The Archaeology of Hindu Ritual (Cambridge, 2009)
sources.note: Standard study of Udayagiri and the Varaha as royal-religious ideology.

episodes.notes: His throne-name was Vikramaditya. The defeat/annexation of the Western Kshatrapas (last king Rudrasimha III) is dated c. 388-409 CE - marked by when Shaka coinage CEASES (~409) and the first Gupta SILVER coins appear. Winning Gujarat's ports fed the prosperity Faxian later saw. The Udayagiri Varaha (Cave 5) equates the king with Vishnu lifting the Earth. The 'Navaratna' (nine gems, incl. Kalidasa) court is LEGENDARY - only in much later texts; present as tradition, not fact. Spoken name should say 'the Second'.

episodes.title: Prabhavatigupta, the Regent Queen
episodes.region: The Vakataka realm of the Deccan (Vidarbha); a daughter of the Gupta house
episodes.logline: Chandragupta II's daughter, widowed young, rules the Deccan for twenty years as regent for her sons, issuing land-grants in her own name and binding the Gupta north to the Vakataka south that would one day paint Ajanta.
episodes.moral: Power held with wisdom can steady a realm as surely as any sword.

sources.citation: Poona and Riddhapur copper-plate grants issued in Prabhavatigupta's own name as regent
sources.note: Primary proof of a Gupta princess exercising sovereign authority.

sources.citation: Vakataka genealogical records; her marriage to Rudrasena II
sources.note: Establishes the Gupta-Vakataka matrimonial alliance.

sources.citation: Hans Bakker, The Vakatakas (1997); Upinder Singh (2008)
sources.note: Scholarship on her regency and the Gupta-Vakataka link to Ajanta.

episodes.notes: She married the Vakataka king Rudrasena II; after his early death she governed as regent (~405-425 CE) for her sons, issuing copper-plate land-grants in HER OWN name - a rare, ATTESTED case of a woman wielding sovereign power in ancient India, and she proudly styles herself of the Gupta (Dharana) gotra. The alliance she embodies connects the Guptas to the later Ajanta caves. A strong female-ruler story (echoing Kundavai in the Chola chapter). Keep it about governance and the alliance, not romance.

episodes.title: Faxian's Road Through a Golden Land
episodes.era: Gupta zenith, the traveller's years c. 399-414 CE
episodes.region: The pilgrim's road from China to Pataliputra and the Gangetic plain
episodes.logline: A Chinese monk walks across the Gupta empire seeking Buddhist scriptures and records what he sees - free hospitals in Pataliputra, orderly cities, gold coins - an outsider's honest snapshot of a golden age, and of whom it truly served.
episodes.moral: A land is judged by how it treats its poorest, not only by the gold in its markets.

sources.citation: Faxian, Foguo Ji (A Record of Buddhist Kingdoms), tr. James Legge (1886)
sources.note: Eyewitness account of Gupta India, incl. the free hospitals of Pataliputra.

sources.citation: R.S. Sharma, Indian Feudalism c. 300-1200 (1965)
sources.note: The land-grant/feudalism critique: prosperity was uneven.

sources.citation: Romila Thapar, Early India (2002); Upinder Singh (2008)
sources.note: Revisionist caution: the 'golden age' as largely an elite experience.

episodes.notes: Faxian (Fa-Hien) travelled c. 399-414 CE and does NOT name the emperor (historians infer Chandragupta II). Use his real observations: charitable hospitals in Pataliputra ('the poor, destitute, crippled and diseased may repair... food and medicine given'); orderly cities; gold dinaras. BUT weave the HONEST nuance the scholarship demands: his 'no poll-tax' likely describes the elite, not all; agrahara land-grants (R.S. Sharma) concentrated land and imposed vishti (forced labour); caste hardened and women's status declined (Thapar, Upinder Singh). Show the brilliance AND the shadow.

episodes.title: Aryabhata Turns the Earth
episodes.era: Gupta science, the Aryabhatiya composed 499 CE
episodes.logline: At twenty-three, Aryabhata writes that the sky only seems to spin because the Earth turns, that eclipses are shadows and not a demon swallowing the sun, and sets down the world's first sine table - the science of a golden age in 121 verses.
episodes.moral: Courage is trusting what reason and measurement reveal, even against old certainties.

sources.citation: Aryabhatiya (499 CE); W.E. Clark (tr.), The Aryabhatiya of Aryabhata (Chicago, 1930); K.V. Sarma ed. (INSA, 1976)
sources.note: The primary text, dated by Aryabhata himself.

sources.citation: Kim Plofker, Mathematics in India (Princeton, 2009)
sources.note: Definitive modern study; clarifies the pi/irrationality question.

sources.citation: Bhaskara I's Aryabhatiya-bhashya, c. 629 CE
sources.note: Earliest commentary.

episodes.notes: Aryabhata (b. 476 CE) wrote the Aryabhatiya in 499 CE at Kusumapura (=Pataliputra). ATTESTED: Earth rotates on its axis (the boat-and-riverbank analogy), lunar/solar eclipses are shadows (rejecting the Rahu/Ketu myth), the first systematic sine (jya) table (24 values), pi = 62832/20000 = 3.1416 which he calls 'asanna' (approximate). IMPORTANT / RECENT SCHOLARSHIP: do NOT claim he PROVED pi irrational - Kim Plofker and Takao Hayashi call that an over-reading; he only called it approximate. He used positional letter-numerals (a step toward place-value) but NO written '0' symbol yet - that comes in the next story. Do not confuse him with the 10th-c. 'Aryabhata II'.
episodes.chapter_part: III. The Age of Number, Iron and Healing

episodes.title: The Dot That Became Zero
episodes.era: The Indian journey of zero, Gupta age to 628 CE
episodes.region: From a birch-bark manuscript in the northwest to Brahmagupta's Bhinmal
episodes.logline: A dot for 'nothing' in an old birch-bark manuscript grows, through Aryabhata's places and Brahmagupta's bold rules of 628, into zero the number - the small circle that would remake the mathematics of the whole world.
episodes.moral: The greatest ideas can begin as almost nothing - a single, patient dot.

sources.citation: Brahmagupta, Brahmasphutasiddhanta (628 CE), ch. 18; tr. H.T. Colebrooke (1817)
sources.note: First systematic arithmetic rules for zero as a number.

sources.citation: The Bakhshali manuscript, Bodleian Library MS Sansk. d.14; T. Hayashi, The Bakhshali Manuscript (1995)
sources.note: Birch-bark handbook using a dot (bindu) placeholder zero.

sources.citation: Kim Plofker, Agathe Keller, Takao Hayashi, Clemency Montelle & Dominik Wujastyk, 'A Response to the Bodleian Library's Radiocarbon Dating', History of Science in South Asia 5/2 (2017)
sources.note: Peer-reviewed rebuttal of the '3rd-century zero' claim.

episodes.notes: Tell the STAGES honestly: Aryabhata's positional logic (499) -> the Bakhshali manuscript's placeholder DOT -> Brahmagupta's Brahmasphutasiddhanta (628 CE) giving zero arithmetic rules (n+0=n, n*0=0; his n/0=0 was the first, imperfect attempt) -> the Gwalior inscription (876 CE), the earliest UNDISPUTED epigraphic '0'. CRUCIAL RECENT SCHOLARSHIP: the 2017 Bodleian radiocarbon claim that Bakhshali's zero is 3rd-4th century was REBUTTED by Plofker, Keller, Hayashi, Montelle & Wujastyk - the bark is old but the MATHS is 7th c.+ (a manuscript is dated by its LATEST folio). Do NOT assert the 3rd-century 'oldest zero' as fact; present the honest, contested story. Brahmagupta (598-668) is just after the imperial Guptas - frame him as the culmination of the golden age's mathematics.
episodes.chapter_part: III. The Age of Number, Iron and Healing

episodes.title: The Iron Pillar That Would Not Rust
episodes.figure: The Master Smith of the Iron Pillar
episodes.era: Gupta metallurgy, c. early 5th century CE
episodes.region: Forged for Vishnupadagiri (Udayagiri); now at Mehrauli, Delhi
episodes.logline: Gupta smiths hammer-weld a hundred glowing blooms into a single seven-metre shaft for King Chandra's Vishnu-standard - and sixteen centuries of monsoons cannot make it rust, a secret modern science has only just explained.
episodes.moral: Mastery of a craft can outlast empires - and even time itself.

sources.citation: The Mehrauli Iron Pillar inscription of King 'Chandra' (Gupta Brahmi); J.F. Fleet, Corpus Inscriptionum Indicarum Vol. III (1888)
sources.note: Names 'Chandra' (widely identified as Chandragupta II) and calls the pillar a Vishnudhvaja.

sources.citation: R. Balasubramaniam, 'On the Corrosion Resistance of the Delhi Iron Pillar', Corrosion Science 42 (2000): 2171-2199
sources.note: Peer-reviewed explanation of the rust resistance.

sources.citation: R. Balasubramaniam, 'On the History of the Delhi Iron Pillar Revealed by Technical Analysis', Indian Journal of History of Science (2007)
sources.note: Forge-welded construction analysis.

episodes.notes: The pillar (~7.2 m, ~6 tonnes) was FORGED, not cast - roughly 20-30 iron blooms hammer-welded together. The inscription names only 'Chandra' (scholarly consensus: Chandragupta II), styles him parama-bhagavata, and calls the pillar a Vishnudhvaja set on Vishnupadagiri (now identified with Udayagiri); it was likely moved to Delhi later. RECENT FINDING (the heart of the story): R. Balasubramaniam (IIT Kanpur, Corrosion Science 2000) showed the rust resistance comes from the ancient iron's HIGH PHOSPHORUS (~0.15%), which in Delhi's wet-dry cycles forms a thin, self-sealing protective layer of iron-phosphate/oxyhydroxide ('misawite') - modern steel burns the phosphorus off. Hero is an anonymous master smith (like the Chola temple architect).
episodes.chapter_part: III. The Age of Number, Iron and Healing

episodes.title: Sushruta and the Healer's Knife
episodes.era: The Gupta redaction of Ayurveda, c. 4th-5th century CE
episodes.region: Ancient Kashi (Varanasi) and the schools of Indian medicine
episodes.logline: In the age when a Chinese pilgrim marvels at India's free hospitals, its surgeons refine the Sushruta Samhita - a hundred and twenty instruments, three hundred operations, and a new nose rebuilt from a patient's own skin, one of the world's first plastic surgeries.
episodes.moral: To heal is the highest craft - knowledge turned to mercy.

sources.citation: Sushruta Samhita (Gupta-era redaction); tr. Kaviraj Kunja Lal Bhishagratna (1907-16)
sources.note: Foundational surgical text: instruments, procedures, rhinoplasty, cataract couching.

sources.citation: G.J. Meulenbeld, A History of Indian Medical Literature (1999-2002)
sources.note: Definitive study of the dating and multi-author redaction of the Ayurvedic corpus.

sources.citation: Faxian, Foguo Ji (c. 414 CE), on the free hospitals of Pataliputra
sources.note: Context for institutional medical charity in Gupta India.

episodes.notes: Be HONEST about dating: the CORE of the Sushruta Samhita is OLDER than the Guptas, but its major REDACTION into canonical form is placed in the Gupta era (c. 3rd-5th c.), and the Caraka Samhita's final redactor Dridhabala is ~4th c. - 'Sushruta' and 'Caraka' are school-founders, not single authors (Meulenbeld). Attested content: ~120 surgical instruments, ~300 procedures, rhinoplasty (forehead/cheek flap - ancestral to modern nose reconstruction, noted in modern plastic-surgery history), cataract couching, obstetrics. Pair with Faxian's real account of free hospitals. Show medicine as an INNOVATION of the age. Keep procedures suggested, not graphic (child-safe).
episodes.chapter_part: III. The Age of Number, Iron and Healing

episodes.title: Nalanda, the Ocean of Learning
episodes.era: Founded under Kumaragupta I, c. 427 CE
episodes.logline: Emperor Kumaragupta endows a monastery in Magadha that grows into Nalanda - thousands of students, a library called the Sea of Gems, and a beacon of learning that will draw seekers from across Asia for seven hundred years.
episodes.moral: The greatest wealth a ruler can leave is a place where knowledge is free to grow.

sources.citation: Xuanzang, Records of the Western Regions (c. 646 CE), tr. Li Rongxi (1996)
sources.note: Eyewitness account of Nalanda's scale, library and curriculum.

sources.citation: Copper-plate and seal references to Nalanda's Gupta foundation under Kumaragupta I
sources.note: Basis for the 5th-century foundation.

sources.citation: UNESCO World Heritage inscription, Archaeological Site of Nalanda Mahavihara, 2016 (List no. 1502)
sources.note: Recent recognition; ASI excavations.

episodes.notes: Founded under Kumaragupta I (5th c., commonly ~427 CE). Xuanzang (7th c.) describes ~10,000 students and 2,000 teachers and the library complex Dharmaganja (Ratnasagara, Ratnodadhi, Ratnaranjaka) - but flag that the 10,000 figure is a single foreign account, possibly peak/rhetorical. RECENT: only ~10% of the site is excavated; UNESCO World Heritage in 2016; newer scholarship suggests Nalanda lingered for decades after Bakhtiyar Khilji's c.1193 attack rather than ending overnight. It taught Buddhist and non-Buddhist thought, logic, medicine, mathematics. Anchor the hero on Kumaragupta I founding it; show the place and its learners. Spoken name should say 'the First'.

episodes.title: Kalidasa and the Cloud Messenger
episodes.era: Classical Sanskrit's peak, c. late 4th-early 5th century CE
episodes.region: Ujjain and the Gupta cultural world
episodes.logline: A poet imagines a lovelorn spirit begging a passing cloud to carry his message home, and writes Shakuntala and the tale of Rama's line - Sanskrit so radiant that, centuries later, Goethe will bow to it.
episodes.moral: Long after empires fall, beauty in words keeps an age alive.

sources.citation: Kalidasa, Abhijnanashakuntalam, Meghaduta, Raghuvamsha, Kumarasambhava; tr. Barbara Stoler Miller, Theater of Memory (1984)
sources.note: The poet's major works.

sources.citation: Aihole inscription (634 CE) naming Kalidasa with Vyasa and Valmiki
sources.note: Confirms his fame as a historical figure by the 7th c.

sources.citation: Sheldon Pollock, The Language of the Gods in the World of Men (2006)
sources.note: Kalidasa within the Gupta 'cosmopolitan Sanskrit' transformation.

episodes.notes: His works: Abhijnanashakuntalam, Meghaduta (the cloud messenger), Raghuvamsha, Kumarasambhava. DATING IS FAMOUSLY UNRESOLVED (proposals span 1st c. BCE-6th c. CE); the defensible CONSENSUS places him late 4th-early 5th c. CE, around Chandragupta II - but the 'Navaratna / nine gems of Vikramaditya's court' link is LEGENDARY (later texts only), so present the court connection as tradition, not fact. The Mandasor inscription (433 CE) may quote him; the Aihole inscription (634 CE) names him. Goethe's praise of Shakuntala put him in the world canon. Age B - gentle and lyrical. Recent feminist readings (Thapar) note Shakuntala both grants and constrains female agency.

episodes.title: The Gods Take Shape at Deogarh
episodes.figure: The Master Sculptor of the Gupta Age
episodes.era: The classical moment of Indian art, 5th-early 6th century CE
episodes.region: Sarnath, Udayagiri and the Dashavatara temple at Deogarh
episodes.logline: Gupta stone-carvers give the Buddha his serene, wet-robed calm at Sarnath and raise Vishnu's temple at Deogarh with its soaring spire - setting the mould for sacred art across Asia for a thousand years.
episodes.moral: A true artist carves not just stone, but the way an age will imagine the divine.

sources.citation: The Sarnath Buddha (5th c.), Archaeological Museum, Sarnath
sources.note: Paradigm of classical Gupta Buddhist sculpture (dharmachakra mudra, transparent robes).

sources.citation: The Dashavatara (Vishnu) temple, Deogarh (early 6th c.); the Udayagiri Varaha relief (c. 401 CE)
sources.note: Early Nagara-style temple with sikhara; the earth-lifting Boar.

sources.citation: Michael Willis, The Archaeology of Hindu Ritual (2009); Walter Spink, Ajanta: History and Development (2005-2014)
sources.note: Recent scholarship on Udayagiri's meaning and Ajanta's compressed chronology.

episodes.notes: The 'classical' Gupta style synthesised the Mathura and Gandhara traditions into a serene, idealised norm copied across South and Southeast Asia. Key works: the Sarnath Buddha (clinging robes, downcast eyes, Wheel-of-Dharma gesture); the Deogarh Dashavatara temple (one of the earliest stone temples with a curvilinear sikhara - foundational for the NAGARA style; its Vishnu-on-the-serpent panel); the Udayagiri Varaha (Vishnu = Chandragupta II lifting the Earth - Michael Willis). RECENT: Walter Spink's compressed chronology dates Ajanta's great late phase to a burst c. 462-480 CE under the Gupta-allied Vakatakas. Hero is an anonymous master sculptor (craft theme, like the Chola bronze artists). Keep divine images reverent and accurate.

episodes.title: Skandagupta Holds Back the Huns
episodes.region: The empire's frontiers; pillars at Bhitari and Girnar (Junagadh)
episodes.logline: The last great Gupta emperor meets the Huna horsemen in a battle that 'makes the earth tremble', repairs the burst Sudarshana dam even as the treasury drains, and holds the golden age together one last time before the long dusk.
episodes.moral: Some victories are measured not in conquest but in how long you hold the light against the dark.

sources.citation: The Bhitari Pillar inscription of Skandagupta
sources.note: Earliest secure Indian source naming the Hunas as invaders; his restoration of the dynasty.

sources.citation: The Junagadh (Girnar) Rock inscription of Skandagupta, Gupta Era 138 (c. 457 CE)
sources.note: Records the repair of the Sudarshana Lake dam under Parnadatta and Chakrapalita.

sources.citation: Encyclopaedia Iranica, 'Hunnic Coinage' (N. Sims-Williams); Upinder Singh (2008)
sources.note: Recent identification of the Hunas as the Alchon Huns.

episodes.notes: Skandagupta (r. c. 455-467) defeated both the internal Pushyamitras and the invading Hunas; the Bhitari pillar (earliest Indian mention of the Hunas) says the encounter 'made the earth tremble'. The Junagadh inscription shows administration still working under strain - his governor Parnadatta and son Chakrapalita repaired the burst Sudarshana Lake dam (the same lake repaired earlier under Rudradaman, 150 CE). RECENT NUMISMATIC SCHOLARSHIP identifies the Hunas as the ALCHON Huns (distinct from the Hephthalites; often conflated in older textbooks). After him the empire fragmented; Toramana and Mihirakula broke it by the early 6th c. Close the chapter on a heroic-but-poignant last stand, and let the age's gifts (numerals, learning, art) be its true legacy.

episodes.title: Chandragupta Raises the Peacock Standard
episodes.era: Founding of the Maurya Empire, c. 321-297 BCE
episodes.region: Magadha, Pataliputra, Punjab and the Gangetic plain
episodes.logline: A young challenger from uncertain origins topples the Nandas, gathers Magadha around Pataliputra, and raises the first empire to bind much of ancient India under one throne.
episodes.moral: A new order can rise when courage is joined to discipline.

sources.citation: Plutarch, Life of Alexander, on 'Androcottus' (Chandragupta)
sources.note: Greek testimony establishing Chandragupta's historical reality outside later Indian legends.

sources.citation: Vishakhadatta, Mudrarakshasa, c. 4th-9th century CE
sources.note: Literary drama of the Nanda overthrow and Chanakya's plotting; useful but late and legendary.

sources.citation: Upinder Singh, A History of Ancient and Early Medieval India (2008); Romila Thapar, Early India (2002)
sources.note: Modern synthesis treating his existence as secure and his origin stories as uncertain.

episodes.notes: Chandragupta founded the Maurya Empire around 321 BCE after overthrowing the last Nanda ruler, traditionally Dhana Nanda, and made Pataliputra the imperial center. KEY ACCURACY FLAGS: his origins are legendary and disputed across Buddhist, Jain and Brahmanical traditions; do NOT present the 'Mura' concubine etymology of Maurya as fact, because modern scholars reject it on Sanskrit grammatical grounds and the Puranas do not mention Mura. The peacock/Moriya connection is more plausible but still belongs to layered tradition. Chanakya's role is central in later storytelling, especially Mudrarakshasa, but there are no Mauryan court records and no contemporary narrative of the Nanda coup.
episodes.chapter_part: I. The Rise of the Peacock Throne

episodes.title: Kautilya's Manual of Spies and Kings
episodes.era: Mauryan statecraft tradition and later Arthashastra compilation
episodes.region: Pataliputra and the imagined machinery of imperial rule
episodes.logline: In a world of forts, treasuries, allies and secret agents, the Arthashastra imagines how a ruler might keep order before the strong swallow the weak.
episodes.moral: Power needs wisdom, restraint and watchfulness to serve the people.

sources.citation: Patrick Olivelle, King, Governance, and Law in Ancient India: Kautilya's Arthasastra (OUP, 2013)
sources.note: Current standard translation and dating discussion.

sources.citation: Thomas R. Trautmann, Kautilya and the Arthasastra: A Statistical Investigation (Brill, 1971)
sources.note: Statistical evidence that the text is composite rather than a single real-time work.

sources.citation: R.P. Kangle, The Kautiliya Arthasastra, 3 vols. (University of Bombay, 1960-65)
sources.note: Classic edition representing the traditional Kautilya attribution.

episodes.notes: Use the Arthashastra's vivid material: the seven limbs of the state, spies disguised as ascetics or merchants, taxation near one-sixth of produce, mines, forts, treasuries and mandala diplomacy. CRITICAL RECENT-SCHOLARSHIP FLAG: do NOT portray the book as Chanakya's real-time notebook written beside Chandragupta. Trautmann found the text statistically heterogeneous; Olivelle places its likely compilation around 50 BCE-50 CE, with possible older Mauryan material and a Kautilya attribution functioning as a traditional nom de plume. It may reflect Mauryan-compatible political thought, but whether its elaborate spy system was actually implemented is uncertain.
episodes.chapter_part: I. The Rise of the Peacock Throne

episodes.title: Five Hundred Elephants for Seleucus
episodes.region: The Indus frontier, Gandhara, Arachosia and the Hindu Kush
episodes.logline: When Seleucus Nicator marches east to reclaim Alexander's frontier, Chandragupta answers with a treaty of lands, marriage ties, an ambassador, and five hundred elephants that shake the Hellenistic world.
episodes.moral: The wisest victories can end in treaties, not endless war.

sources.citation: Appian, Syriaca 55
sources.note: Primary Greek account of Seleucus crossing the Indus, making treaty and contracting a marriage alliance with Sandrocottus.

sources.citation: Strabo, Geographica XV.2.9
sources.note: Greek source for the marriage alliance and ceded eastern satrapies.

sources.citation: Paul J. Kosmin, The Land of the Elephant Kings (Harvard University Press, 2014)
sources.note: Modern study of Seleucid space, diplomacy and the eastern frontier.

episodes.notes: The treaty after the Seleucid-Mauryan war gave Chandragupta control of Gandhara, Arachosia, Gedrosia and the Paropamisadae, while Seleucus received 500 war elephants and sent Megasthenes to Pataliputra. The elephants later mattered at Ipsus in 301 BCE. ACCURACY FLAGS: the exact war and treaty dates fall in a debated 305-303 BCE range, with treaty dates sometimes placed as late as 303-298 BCE; the marriage alliance is real in Greek sources, but epigamia may mean formal intermarriage or permission for elite intermarriage, so do NOT invent a named Seleucid princess romance. The Kandahar Greek Edict under Ashoka later confirms Mauryan control in the region.
episodes.chapter_part: II. The Empire Meets the World

episodes.title: Megasthenes at the Wooden Capital
episodes.era: Greek embassy to Chandragupta's court, c. 300 BCE
episodes.region: Pataliputra, at the ancient Ganges-Son river system
episodes.logline: A Greek ambassador walks the long timber walls of Pataliputra, counting towers, gates and river defenses, while trying to describe an India his own world barely understands.
episodes.moral: A stranger's account can illuminate history, but must be read with care.

sources.citation: Megasthenes, Indica, preserved in Arrian, Strabo, Diodorus, Pliny and Aelian
sources.note: Lost eyewitness account surviving only as later Greek and Roman fragments.

sources.citation: ASI excavations at Bulandibagh and Kumrahar, Patna (Waddell 1895; Spooner 1912-13; Jaiswal 1951-55)
sources.note: Wooden palisade beams and the 80-pillar hall evidence tied to ancient Pataliputra.

sources.citation: Richard Stoneman, The Greek Experience of India (Princeton University Press, 2019)
sources.note: Recent assessment of Greek knowledge and Megasthenes' reliability.

episodes.notes: Megasthenes' Indica is lost and survives only in fragments quoted by later authors; Strabo called him unreliable, while modern scholars use him carefully. Tell the physical city: a parallelogram-like Pataliputra, wooden palisade with arrow slits, ditch, 570 towers and 64 gates, and a palace compared with Susa and Ecbatana. Archaeology at Bulandibagh found waterlogged wooden palisade beams, and Kumrahar revealed remains of a pillared hall, though the exact 80-pillar reconstruction and function remain debated. RECENT GUIDANCE: the Son River shifted course; satellite and geomorphological studies support the ancient confluence context. Do NOT present Megasthenes' 'seven castes' or fabulous descriptions as accurate Indian social science.
episodes.chapter_part: II. The Empire Meets the World

episodes.title: Bindusara's Figs Wine and Philosopher
episodes.era: The transitional Mauryan reign, c. 297-272 BCE
episodes.region: Pataliputra, the Deccan traditions, and the Seleucid court of Antiochus I
episodes.logline: Between Chandragupta and Ashoka, Bindusara keeps the empire steady and sends west for sweet wine, dried figs and a philosopher the Greeks refuse to export.
episodes.moral: Quiet stewardship can matter as much as dazzling conquest.

sources.citation: Athenaeus, Deipnosophistai, on Bindusara's request to Antiochus I
sources.note: Greek anecdote of sweet wine, dried figs and a sophist.

sources.citation: Puranic king-lists; Divyavadana and Mahavamsa traditions
sources.note: Later textual evidence for succession and family traditions.

sources.citation: Upinder Singh, A History of Ancient and Early Medieval India (2008); Paul Kosmin, The Land of the Elephant Kings (2014)
sources.note: Modern chronology and context for Seleucid-Mauryan diplomatic ties.

episodes.notes: Bindusara, Chandragupta's son and Ashoka's father, is epigraphically invisible: no inscription from his reign has been found. Greek sources preserve his name as Amitrochates/Allitrochades, reflecting Amitraghata, 'slayer of enemies.' The famous letter to Antiochus I asking for sweet wine, dried figs and a sophist is a wonderful scene, but Athenaeus wrote centuries later; treat the diplomatic exchange as plausible and the sophist detail as possibly literary. Taranatha's much later claim that Bindusara conquered the land between the two seas is disputed, so do NOT map him as proven conqueror of peninsular India. He is best shown as the shadowed consolidator between two better-attested rulers.
episodes.chapter_part: II. The Empire Meets the World

episodes.title: Ashoka Appoints the Dhamma Officers
episodes.region: Across the Mauryan provinces from Pataliputra to the frontiers
episodes.logline: Ashoka sends officials across the roads and provinces, asking an empire of many faiths to practice mercy, restraint, respect and care for the vulnerable.
episodes.moral: A ruler's duty is not only to command, but to care.

sources.citation: Ashoka's Rock Edicts 2, 3, 5 and 7; E. Hultzsch, Inscriptions of Asoka (1925)
sources.note: Primary evidence for roads, welfare, mahamatras, dhamma-mahamatras and respect for sects.

sources.citation: Ashoka's Rock Edict 13 and Pillar Edict 7
sources.note: Dhamma conquest, moral works and the king's summary of policy; avoid retelling Kalinga here.

sources.citation: Romila Thapar, Asoka and the Decline of the Mauryas (1961; 3rd ed. 1997); Nayanjot Lahiri, Ashoka in Ancient India (2015)
sources.note: Modern interpretation of dhamma as imperial ethic as well as religious conviction.

episodes.notes: This story must NOT retell the existing flagship Kalinga change-of-heart episode. Focus on policy: dhamma as non-violence, respect for all sects, obedience to parents, generosity, humane treatment of servants, welfare for prisoners and women, wells, shade trees and medical care. Rock Edict 5 creates dhamma-mahamatras, a new class of morality/welfare officers with no exact Arthashastra parallel. CRITICAL ACCURACY: Ashoka's dhamma is not identical to Buddhist doctrine; Greek versions translate it as eusebeia, piety, and Thapar reads it as a non-sectarian imperial ideology. Recent scholarship debates sincere Buddhist faith versus pragmatic imperial glue; present both as compatible rather than reducing him to either saint or politician.

episodes.title: Prinsep Reads the Forgotten King
episodes.region: Calcutta, Sanchi, Bharhut, Maski and the Ashokan inscription network
episodes.logline: After a thousand years of silence, James Prinsep notices tiny repeated letters for 'gift' and unlocks Brahmi, revealing the voice of Devanampiya Piyadasi as Ashoka.
episodes.moral: Patience can make even silent stones speak again.

sources.citation: Ashoka's rock and pillar edicts; E. Hultzsch, Inscriptions of Asoka (1925)
sources.note: The physical inscriptions in Brahmi, Kharoshthi, Greek and Aramaic.

sources.citation: James Prinsep, articles in Journal of the Asiatic Society of Bengal, 1836-1838
sources.note: Published decipherment work on Brahmi and related inscriptions.

sources.citation: Richard Salomon, Indian Epigraphy (OUP, 1998); Harry Falk, Asokan Sites and Artefacts (2006)
sources.note: Modern epigraphic assessment and site catalogue.

episodes.notes: Ashoka's edicts became unreadable after Brahmi and Kharoshthi fell out of use. Prinsep, secretary of the Asiatic Society of Bengal, cracked Brahmi by studying repeated short Sanchi and Bharhut donation inscriptions ending in danam. By March 1838 his Brahmi alphabet was nearly complete, but the edict king called Devanampiya Piyadasi was linked to Ashoka only after George Turnour's Pali Mahavamsa evidence; Christian Lassen's 1836 coin work and Alexander Cunningham's help belong in the collaborative story. The Maski edict found in 1915 later confirmed the identification by naming Ashoka. RECENT/ACCURACY FLAGS: more than 30 edicts survive; Kandahar's Greek-Aramaic bilingual inscription was discovered in 1958 and proves a multilingual Mauryan frontier. Do not make Prinsep a lone genius without the network.

episodes.title: The Four Lions of Sarnath
episodes.era: Ashokan pillar art, c. 250 BCE and modern national adoption in 1947-1950
episodes.region: Sarnath near Varanasi; Chunar sandstone routes; the Sarnath Museum
episodes.logline: A pillar raised where the Buddha first taught carries four roaring lions, a shattered wheel, and a polished message that one day becomes India's national emblem.
episodes.moral: Symbols endure when they speak to justice beyond one age.

sources.citation: Sarnath Lion Capital of Ashoka, excavated by F.O. Oertel, ASI, 1904-1905
sources.note: Physical monolithic Chunar sandstone capital, now in the Sarnath Museum.

sources.citation: Ashokan pillars and pillar edicts; Ashoka's own term dhamma-thambha
sources.note: Primary context for pillars as carriers of dhamma.

sources.citation: Frederick Asher, Sarnath: Buddhism, Art and Archives (2020); Vidya Dehejia, Indian Art (1997); John Boardman, The Diffusion of Classical Art in Antiquity (1994)
sources.note: Recent and classic scholarship on Sarnath, Mauryan polish and Achaemenid/Hellenistic influence.

episodes.notes: The Sarnath Lion Capital is 2.1 m high, carved from a single block of Chunar sandstone, with lotus base, animal abacus, four back-to-back lions and an originally large Dharma Wheel. It was excavated in broken pieces in 1904-05; the pillar shaft stayed in situ. The Mauryan polish should gleam visually, but its technique remains uncertain. KEY ART-HISTORY FLAG: do not frame it as simply Persian copying or purely isolated Indian invention; current consensus is synthesis, with Achaemenid/Hellenistic craft influence combined with Indian Buddhist symbolism. Nehru proposed the Ashoka Chakra for the flag in July 1947, and the Lion Capital became India's State Emblem on January 26, 1950, with Satyameva Jayate added below.

episodes.title: Mahinda's Bodhi Sapling Voyage
episodes.era: Buddhist missions after the Third Council, c. 250-247 BCE
episodes.region: Pataliputra, Mihintale and Anuradhapura in Sri Lanka
episodes.logline: From Ashoka's court, Mahinda carries teaching to Mihintale while Sanghamitra brings a Bodhi sapling to Anuradhapura, planting a living bridge between India and Sri Lanka.
episodes.moral: A peaceful mission can change the memory of nations.

sources.citation: Dipavamsa and Mahavamsa, Sri Lankan Pali chronicles
sources.note: Main narrative sources for the Third Council, Mahinda, Sanghamitra and the Bodhi sapling.

sources.citation: Rajagala inscription, Sri Lanka, c. 200 BCE
sources.note: Early Brahmi epigraphic corroboration of Mahinda's presence in Sri Lanka.

sources.citation: Nayanjot Lahiri, Ashoka in Ancient India (Harvard University Press, 2015); Wilhelm Geiger trans., Mahavamsa (1908)
sources.note: Modern and classic handling of the missions tradition.

episodes.notes: This Ashoka story should be about missions, not Kalinga. According to Sri Lankan chronicles, Ashoka convened the Third Buddhist Council at Pataliputra under Moggaliputta Tissa and then supported missions; Mahinda met King Devanampiya Tissa at Mihintale, and Sanghamitra brought a cutting from the Bodhi tree to Anuradhapura. The Sri Maha Bodhi is revered as the oldest continuously documented tree, though no destructive scientific dating is published. CRITICAL FLAGS: the chronicles are centuries later and sectarian; the Rajagala inscription is the strongest independent evidence for Mahinda's mission; Mahinda as Ashoka's biological son is accepted by some modern scholars but remains debated against honorific 'dharma-son' readings. Do not overclaim the full council narrative as contemporary fact.

episodes.title: The Echoing Caves for Ajivikas
episodes.era: Barabar and Nagarjuni cave dedications, c. 259-245 BCE and after
episodes.logline: Ashoka cuts mirror-bright chambers into hard granite for the Ajivikas, where every whisper blooms into an echo and stone begins India's rock-cut architecture.
episodes.moral: True tolerance builds shelters even for paths not one's own.

sources.citation: Ashoka's Barabar cave inscriptions, including Sudama Cave year 12 and Karan Chaupar year 19
sources.note: Dated primary dedications to the Ajivikas.

sources.citation: Dasharatha Maurya's Nagarjuni Hill cave inscriptions
sources.note: Post-Ashokan Mauryan dedications continuing the cave tradition.

sources.citation: Harry Falk, Asokan Sites and Artefacts (2006); James C. Harle, The Art and Architecture of the Indian Subcontinent (1994)
sources.note: Epigraphic and art-historical context for the earliest rock-cut caves.

episodes.notes: Barabar Hill contains Sudama, Lomas Rishi, Karan Chaupar and Visvakarma caves; Sudama, dedicated by Ashoka in his 12th regnal year, is the earliest dated rock-cut cave in India. The caves' polished interiors and curved rooms create the famous echoes that inspired E.M. Forster's fictional Marabar Caves. Their Ajivika dedication is vital: Ashoka was Buddhist but patronized a heterodox determinist ascetic sect, demonstrating pluralism. RECENT FLAGS: Harry Falk's re-reading clarified Karan Chaupar's Ajivika dedication; Lomas Rishi lacks an Ashokan inscription and may be unfinished or post-Ashokan. S.P. Gupta's theory that Lomas Rishi was halted around Pushyamitra's coup is intriguing but debated, not fact.

episodes.title: The Didarganj Yakshi's Polished Secret
episodes.era: Early historic sculpture, debated Mauryan or Kushan date
episodes.region: Didarganj near Patna; Bihar Museum, Patna
episodes.logline: A riverbank reveals a shining yakshi with a fly-whisk, but her mirror polish hides a mystery: is she Mauryan splendor or a later Kushan masterpiece?
episodes.moral: Honest wonder leaves room for unanswered questions.

sources.citation: Didarganj Yakshi, found at Didarganj Kadam Rasual near Patna on October 18, 1917
sources.note: Physical Chunar sandstone sculpture with famous mirror-like polish, now in Bihar Museum.

sources.citation: James C. Harle, The Art and Architecture of the Indian Subcontinent (1994)
sources.note: Art-historical basis for questioning the traditional Mauryan date.

sources.citation: Bihar Museum, Patna, updated early historic display interpretation
sources.note: Current cautious museum framing after the sculpture's move from Patna Museum.

episodes.notes: The Didarganj Yakshi is a life-sized Chunar sandstone female figure holding a chauri, discovered by villagers in 1917 and recovered by J.N. Samaddar and D.B. Spooner. It has long been celebrated as Mauryan because of its spectacular polish, but modern art historians including Harle and Rowland argue it may be Kushan, 1st-2nd century CE, based on forelock, ornament, drapery and comparison with other yakshis. CRITICAL ACCURACY: do NOT say Mauryan polish alone proves Mauryan date; the technique persisted after the Mauryas. Mention the 1985-86 Smithsonian travel damage to the nose only as a patrimony caution if needed. Best story angle: the sculpture teaches how art history weighs style, material and provenance rather than forcing certainty.

episodes.title: The Parade That Ended the Mauryas
episodes.era: End of the Maurya Empire, c. 185 BCE
episodes.region: Pataliputra and the fractured post-Ashokan empire
episodes.logline: At a military review, the general Pushyamitra kills the last Mauryan king Brihadratha, and the empire of Chandragupta and Ashoka gives way to the Shungas.
episodes.moral: An empire that cannot renew its bonds may fall from within.

sources.citation: Bana, Harshacharita, 7th century CE
sources.note: Later literary source for Pushyamitra assassinating Brihadratha during a military parade.

sources.citation: Ayodhya Inscription of Dhana
sources.note: Epigraphic evidence for Pushyamitra and his Ashvamedha sacrifices.

sources.citation: Romila Thapar, Asoka and the Decline of the Mauryas (1961; 3rd ed. 1997); Upinder Singh (2008)
sources.note: Modern analysis of decline, fragmentation and the persecution debate.

episodes.notes: After Ashoka, the king-list becomes poorly attested: Kunala/Dasharatha, Samprati, Shalishuka, Devavarman, Shatadhanvan and Brihadratha are known mostly from Puranic lists with differing details. Pushyamitra's coup around 185 BCE is the accepted end of Mauryan rule, but Bana's assassination scene is a 7th-century literary account, not an eyewitness report. ACCURACY FLAGS: do not blame the fall simplistically on Ashoka's pacifism; historians also cite over-extension, loose network control, economic strain, succession fragmentation and external Indo-Greek pressure. Buddhist texts accuse Pushyamitra of persecution, but Thapar and others stress the lack of firm archaeological proof, Roman denarii anachronisms in Ashokavadana, and Shunga-period expansion at Bharhut and Sanchi. Show a contested, multifactor decline.

episodes.title: Chandragupta's Last Fast at Shravanabelagola
episodes.era: Digambara Jain tradition of Chandragupta's final years, after c. 297 BCE
episodes.logline: In Jain memory, the conqueror lays down his crown, follows Bhadrabahu south, and ends his life in silent fasting on Chandragiri Hill.
episodes.moral: The greatest conquest may be mastery over oneself.

sources.citation: Harishena, Brihakatha Kosha (931 CE); later Bhadrabahu Charita and Jain traditions
sources.note: Late Digambara textual tradition for abdication, migration and sallekhana.

sources.citation: Shravanabelagola inscriptions from c. 600 CE onward linking Bhadrabahu and Chandragupta
sources.note: Late epigraphic evidence that the tradition existed at the site.

sources.citation: Sushma Jansari on Chandragupta and Bhadrabahu (2023); Paul Dundas, The Jains (2002); R.K. Mookerji, Chandragupta Maurya and His Times (1966)
sources.note: Modern critical analysis of the late and problematic evidence.

episodes.notes: Tell this explicitly as Digambara Jain tradition, not settled history. The story says Chandragupta abdicated to Bindusara, became a monk under Bhadrabahu, migrated during a 12-year famine to Shravanabelagola, and performed sallekhana on Chandragiri Hill. CRITICAL RECENT SCHOLARSHIP: Sushma Jansari (2023) argues the evidence is late and problematic, with no non-Jain support and no 3rd-century BCE corroboration; the earliest inscriptional link appears around 600 CE, roughly 900 years after the supposed events. There may be identity confusion with Prabhacandra or Samprati Chandragupta, and Svetambara tradition rejects the southern migration. The site and tradition are real, and Chandragupta's actual end is unknown, so frame it as a powerful remembered ending with clear caveats.

episodes.title: Harihara and Bukka Raise a City of Victory
episodes.figure: Harihara I and Bukka Raya I
episodes.era: Foundation of Vijayanagara, traditional date 1336 CE
episodes.region: Hampi, the Tungabhadra valley, Kampili, Hoysala country and Sringeri
episodes.logline: Two Sangama brothers turn a boulder-ringed river valley into Vijayanagara, a new capital whose origin story blends inscription, politics and sacred memory.
episodes.moral: Great beginnings are strongest when legend is balanced with evidence.

sources.citation: Sringeri Grant of 1346 CE; South Indian Inscriptions series
sources.note: Earliest hard epigraphic evidence for Harihara's sovereignty and the capital's early name.

sources.citation: Burton Stein, Vijayanagara, New Cambridge History of India I.2 (1989)
sources.note: Modern synthesis of the empire's rise and political structure.

sources.citation: Rao, Shulman and Subrahmanyam, Textures of Time (2003)
sources.note: Treats the conversion-and-return story as a legitimizing foundation tradition.

episodes.notes: Harihara I and Bukka Raya I, sons of Sangama, are securely tied to the empire's foundation, with 1336 widely accepted as the traditional date and 1346 giving the earliest firm inscriptional confirmation. KEY FACTS: early evidence points to Hoysala and Kampili political worlds, not a single simple origin; Vidyaranya and Sringeri mattered for legitimacy, but Vidyaranya became Sringeri pontiff only later, so do NOT show him as a fully proven mastermind personally reconverting the brothers in 1336. RECENT SCHOLARSHIP stresses gradual consolidation by local powers, not an instant religious revival. Accuracy flag for the whole chapter: include the four dynasties, Sangama, Saluva, Tuluva and Aravidu, and avoid framing Vijayanagara simply as a Hindu bulwark against Islam.
episodes.chapter_part: I. The City of Victory Rises

episodes.title: Hampi the Boulder Crowned Capital
episodes.era: Vijayanagara capital at its height, 14th-16th centuries CE
episodes.region: Sacred Centre, Royal Centre and agricultural zones of Hampi, Karnataka
episodes.logline: Among granite hills and the Tungabhadra, Hampi grows into a sacred, royal and agricultural city whose streets astonish visitors from Persia and Portugal.
episodes.moral: A city can be designed as both home and sacred landscape.

sources.citation: Abdur Razzaq, Matla-us-Sadain, 1443
sources.note: Persian ambassador's account of seven walls, gardens and unmatched urban splendour.

sources.citation: Domingo Paes, Narrative, c. 1520-22, in Robert Sewell, A Forgotten Empire (1900)
sources.note: Portuguese eyewitness comparison of the city to Rome.

sources.citation: Fritz and Michell, Hampi Vijayanagara (Marg, 2003)
sources.note: Vijayanagara Research Project documentation of sacred, royal and agrarian zones.

episodes.notes: Hampi's core stretched across roughly 25-33 sq. km in a granite boulder landscape, with the Virupaksha sacred centre, the Royal Centre, Mahanavami Dibba, elephant stables, markets and an agricultural buffer. RECENT FINDINGS: the Vijayanagara Research Project, directed by John M. Fritz and George Michell from 1980 to about 2012, mapped more than 1,600 standing or ruined structures and many minor features, creating the benchmark archaeological atlas. Razzaq's seven walls and Paes's Rome comparison are powerful foreign impressions, but do NOT treat every number or comparison as census-grade fact. The Kishkindha associations are sacred geography, not historical geography, even though they shaped the site's meaning.
episodes.chapter_part: I. The City of Victory Rises

episodes.title: The Tanks That Fed an Empire
episodes.figure: Kamalapura Tank and the Bukka Aqueduct
episodes.era: Vijayanagara hydraulic works, especially 15th-16th centuries CE
episodes.region: Kamalapura, Anegundi, the Royal Centre and Tungabhadra canals
episodes.logline: In a dry stone landscape, tanks, aqueducts, pipes and channels carry water into fields, palaces and rituals, letting a vast capital survive.
episodes.moral: Civilization depends on patient care for water.

sources.citation: Kamalapura Tank inscription, 15th century
sources.note: Describes the large perennial tank that watered the suburb and fields.

sources.citation: ASI Hampi excavation reports, 1970s-2020s
sources.note: Excavation evidence for terracotta pipes, channels, drains and water structures.

sources.citation: Nagaveni A., Tanks of Kamalapura, AIIR Journal (2018)
sources.note: Recent study of tank construction phases and inscriptional record.

episodes.notes: Vijayanagara received limited rainfall, so its survival depended on Kamalapura Tank, the Bukka Aqueduct near Anegundi, rock-cut channels, sluices, terracotta pipes, stepped tanks and agricultural canals. KEY FACTS: Kamalapura irrigated fields and fed the Royal Centre; the Bukka Aqueduct is a standing stone-channel system; underground pipes near royal buildings show concealed plumbing. RECENT FINDINGS: 2026 ASI work near Hazara Rama reported stone drainage channels, a 23-metre canal and related remains, extending the water map into the ceremonial core. Do NOT state peak population figures like 500,000-750,000 as census facts; they are estimates from foreign accounts and urban footprint.
episodes.chapter_part: I. The City of Victory Rises

episodes.title: Krishnadevaraya Takes Raichur
episodes.era: Tuluva dynasty zenith and the Raichur campaign, 1520 CE
episodes.region: Raichur Doab between the Krishna and Tungabhadra rivers; Vijayanagara and Bijapur
episodes.logline: Krishnadevaraya marches on Raichur with nayaka troops, elephants and Portuguese guns, winning a dazzling victory that also hardens Deccan rivalries.
episodes.moral: Triumph needs humility, because victory can plant future danger.

sources.citation: Domingo Paes, Narrative, c. 1520-22, in Sewell, A Forgotten Empire (1900)
sources.note: Eyewitness-era Portuguese account of Krishnadevaraya and the Raichur campaign.

sources.citation: Ferishta, Tarikh-i-Ferishta, c. 1612, trans. Briggs (1829)
sources.note: Deccan Persian perspective on Raichur and the 'kiss my foot' episode.

sources.citation: Richard M. Eaton, 'Kiss My Foot,' Said the King, Modern Asian Studies
sources.note: Recent analysis of firearms, diplomacy and Raichur's long-term consequences.

episodes.notes: Krishnadevaraya ruled 1509-1529 and captured Raichur Fort from Ismail Adil Shah in 1520, using a cosmopolitan army that included nayaka contingents, Muslim cavalrymen and Portuguese gunners. KEY ACCURACY: Paes's huge army figures are foreign estimates and almost certainly exaggerated; show scale without repeating them as literal numbers. Eaton's recent scholarship reframes the battle as military theatre and diplomatic overreach, with firearms and the humiliating 'kiss my foot' demand helping sour later Deccan politics. Do NOT stage this as a Hindu army versus a Muslim enemy; Vijayanagara and the Sultanates shared soldiers, technology, diplomacy and shifting alliances.
episodes.chapter_part: II. The Zenith of the Rayas

episodes.title: The King Who Wrote Amuktamalyada
episodes.region: Vijayanagara court, Telugu literary world, Srirangam and Pandharpur devotional imagination
episodes.logline: The warrior king becomes a poet, composing Amuktamalyada and gathering Telugu masters whose fame later shines as the Ashtadiggajas.
episodes.moral: The finest rulers honor both sword and song.

sources.citation: Krishnadevaraya, Amuktamalyada, Telugu manuscripts
sources.note: Primary literary evidence for the king as author.

sources.citation: Rao and Shulman, trans., Krishnadevaraya's Amuktamalyada (Penguin Classics India, 2021)
sources.note: Recent accessible scholarly translation and interpretation.

sources.citation: Subrahmanyam, Shulman and Rao, Textures of Time (2003)
sources.note: Explains literary patronage as political culture.

episodes.notes: Amuktamalyada tells the story of Andal and Ranganatha while embedding ideas of kingship, devotion and governance. KEY FACTS: Krishnadevaraya's authorship is attested by manuscripts and internal tradition; Allasani Peddana, Nandi Thimmana, Dhurjati, Pingali Surana and others were real poets in the Telugu golden age.
